ai.0: Everything in Unit 3 Area of Study 2 — and most of the year beyond it — grows from one question: how can an object move another object it never touches? Read this story in one sitting. Nothing here needs to be solved, only seen.
ai.1: Let go of your phone and it falls. Nothing visible reaches up to take it — no string, no hand, no puff of air — yet the instant your fingers open, something accelerates it toward the floor at 9.8 m s⁻², every time.
ai.2: A compass needle, sealed inside its plastic case, swings to the north wherever in Victoria you stand. A balloon rubbed on a jumper lifts your hair from a handsbreadth away. Three small mysteries, one shared puzzle: in each case, motion arrives with no contact at all.
ai.3: This puzzle has bothered better minds than ours. Newton wrote the law of gravitation that still guides spacecraft, yet he privately called the notion of bodies acting on one another across empty space an absurdity, and declined to guess how the trick was done. The whole of this Area of Study — its official title is How do things move without contact? — is the modern reply.
ai.4: The reply is a single idea, the field, plus a year of consequences. This page tells the story end to end, so that when the detail arrives in Pass 2 it arrives in a landscape you already know.
ai.5: Connects You have been using a field strength since Year 11: the 9.8 you multiply masses by carries the unit N kg⁻¹ — newtons of force for every kilogram placed at that point. Pass 2 chapter 4 makes that unit the centre of Unit 3.
ai.6: The answer arrived from an unlikely direction. Michael Faraday — a bookbinder's apprentice who taught himself science between jobs — spent the 1830s watching iron filings arrange themselves into arcs around magnets. Where others saw a pretty pattern, Faraday saw something bolder: the magnet alters the space around it, and that altered condition of space — he called the region a field — is real, present whether or not anything is there to be acted on.
ai.7: The modern definition keeps his idea and tightens it: a field is a physical quantity that has a value at every point in space. Some fields store a single number at each point — a sports analyst's heat map, recording how often a player occupied each patch of the MCG, is exactly this kind of field.
ai.8: The fields in this Area of Study store something richer at every point: an arrow. The arrow's direction tells you which way the force exerted on a suitable test object placed there would act, and its length tells you how strongly. A field diagram, with its smooth lines and arrowheads, is a map of those arrows — direction along the line, strength shown by how closely the lines crowd together.
ai.9: One drawing rule does real work: field lines never cross. If two lines met, the field at that point would have two directions at once — and no force can act in two directions at once.
ai.10: Connects A field diagram is your Unit 2 force diagram scaled up: instead of drawing the arrows acting on one object, you pre-compute the arrow for every point in space. The precise definition, test objects and field-line rules open the core at What is a field?
ai.11: Unit 3 hands you three of these maps. The gravitational field is sourced by mass and acts on mass. The electric field is sourced by charge and acts on charge. The magnetic field is sourced by moving charge — a current in a wire, or the orbiting and spinning electrons inside a bar magnet — and acts on moving charge. Figure 1.1 draws the three side by side, and the family resemblance is the point: one grammar, three dialects.
ai.12: Now the differences, because the differences carry the physics. Mass comes in one kind only — there is no negative mass — so gravity only ever attracts, and every gravitational field line points inward. In the language of Figure 1.1, a mass is purely a sink: lines flow in, never out.
ai.13: Electric charge comes in two signs. Field lines flow out of positive charges (sources) and into negative charges (sinks), and either kind can exist on its own — an isolated charge is called a monopole. Two signs means electric forces can attract or repel.
ai.14: Magnetism follows the strangest rule of the three. Nobody has ever found an isolated north or south pole: cut a bar magnet in half and you hold two complete magnets, each with both poles, all the way down to subatomic particles. Magnetic sources are dipoles only — a source and a sink permanently paired — which is why the magnet's lines in Figure 1.1 leave the north face and loop around to the south.
ai.15: Hold that three-way contrast — gravity: monopoles of one sign; electricity: monopoles of both signs; magnetism: dipoles only — and the three maps stop being three things to memorise and become one idea with three settings. Simulation 1.1 lets you test all three for yourself.
ai.16: Connects This comparison becomes an examinable table — sources, directions, shapes, attraction and repulsion, monopoles and dipoles — in Comparing the three fields, and the exam harvests it dot point by dot point in Pass 3.
ai.17: Here is the first of four threads to carry through the year. Lift your schoolbag from the floor to the desk and you have done work against the gravitational field. That energy has not vanished: it is stored in the bag's raised position, and the moment the bag slides off the desk it is returned as kinetic energy, in full.
ai.18: Every field in this Area of Study runs the same bank. Separate a positive and a negative charge and you bank energy in their separation; release them and the account empties back into motion. Position in a field is stored energy — potential energy — and the whole topic is double-entry bookkeeping: energy out of one account, into another, total unchanged.
ai.19: The accounting unit is the detail to watch. Gravitational stores are tallied per kilogram of mass; electrical stores are tallied per coulomb of charge — and "energy per coulomb" is a quantity you have used since Unit 1 under its everyday name, voltage. One formula, W = qV not on sheet, says exactly this: the energy transformed when a charge q moves through a potential difference V — the formula sheet carries the same statement folded into ½mv² = qV. Nothing to calculate yet — only notice that it is Unit 1's definition of voltage read backwards.
ai.20: And when a field is not uniform, the store is read graphically, as the area under a force–distance graph — the same trick Units 1&2 used for springs. One ledger, new deposit types.
ai.21: Connects Gravitational storage is banked in Gravity and orbits and electrical storage in Electric fields. Area of Study 1 runs the identical ledger for projectiles and collisions, and in Unit 4 the photoelectric effect will use voltage as a measuring instrument for single electrons.
ai.22: Thread two is a shape. Newton imagined a cannon on a mountaintop above the atmosphere, fired horizontally. A slow cannonball arcs to the ground nearby; a faster one lands further away; and at about 7.9 km s⁻¹ something remarkable happens — the ball falls toward Earth exactly as fast as Earth's surface curves away beneath it. It is falling continuously, and missing continuously: that is all an orbit is.
ai.23: The Moon is Newton's cannonball, fired long ago. In every second it falls about 1.4 mm toward Earth — the distance its curved path drops below the straight line it would otherwise follow — and in that same second its sideways velocity carries it far enough that the drop never brings it closer. A satellite needs no engine to stay up; it needs sideways speed and a single inward force: gravity.
ai.24: This rewrites what you may believe about astronauts. At the International Space Station's altitude the gravitational field strength is still about 8.7 N kg⁻¹ — close to 90 per cent of its surface value — so "there is no gravity in space" is wrong. Astronauts float because the station and everyone inside it are falling together: no surface exerts a normal force on anyone, and the normal force is what is felt as weight. Physicists call the state apparent weightlessness, and the word "apparent" is doing honest work.
ai.25: Now the costume change. A charged particle moving across a magnetic field experiences a force, named by F = qvB on formula sheet, that points at right angles to the particle's velocity — always. A sideways force of constant size changes a path's direction but never its speed, so the particle is steered into a circle, exactly as the satellite is. Whether the inward role is played by gravity on a satellite or by a magnetic field on an electron in a synchrotron ring, it is one piece of circular-motion mathematics in two costumes — constant speed, never constant velocity, because the direction never stops changing.
ai.26: Connects Orbits are built properly in Satellites and circling charges in Circular paths. Area of Study 1 supplies the circular-motion rule both obey — learn to spot which force is playing the inward, centripetal role and half the year's hardest problems collapse into one.
ai.27: Thread three: fields are not only for explaining nature — you can build with them. Start from the workhorse fact of this Area of Study: a current-carrying wire placed across a magnetic field experiences a force, named by F = nIlB on formula sheet. Reverse the current and the force reverses too.
ai.28: Now bend the wire into a rectangular coil on an axle between two magnet poles. Current travels up one side of the coil and down the other, so the two sides experience equal forces in opposite directions — a turning pair, and the coil rotates. There is one flaw in the design: half a turn later, the same pair of forces would turn it back the other way.
ai.29: The fix is a small piece of genius called the split-ring commutator — a copper ring cut in two that reverses the current through the coil every half-turn, so the turning effect always acts the same way. That is a DC motor, the machine inside every drill, fan and electric vehicle, and a diagram you will come to know intimately. Run the film backwards — spin the coil with wind, water or steam — and a current is driven through the wire instead: that machine is a generator, and it is the whole subject of Area of Study 3.
ai.30: The other great field machine is the particle accelerator, whose logic is a two-line summary of this Area of Study: a uniform electric field adds kinetic energy to a charge along a straight line, and a uniform magnetic field bends the charge's path without changing its speed. The Australian Synchrotron in Clayton chains the two together, holding electrons at more than 99.99 per cent of the speed of light.
ai.31: Connects The motor — with the labelled split-ring commutator diagram examiners ask about almost every year — is built in Motors & accelerators. Area of Study 3 will run the identical hardware in reverse to light the state.
ai.32: The last thread points past the horizon of this Area of Study. Among its dot points is a small act of sorting: classify every field as static or changing, and as uniform or non-uniform. It looks like housekeeping; it is the most consequential classification of the year.
ai.33: Changing magnetic fields drive currents in nearby conductors — that single fact, Faraday's discovery of induction, is how almost every power station on Earth generates electricity, and it is Area of Study 3 in one sentence. Unit 4 then takes the idea somewhere astonishing: a changing electric field creates a changing magnetic field, which recreates the electric field, and the self-sustaining pair detaches from charges and wires altogether and travels through empty space. That travelling ripple of fields is light. The field, invented as a bookkeeping device for forces, turns out to be a thing in its own right.
ai.34: A second loose thread is printed in the study design itself: the circular-path rule for charges is guaranteed only while the speed v is much less than c. Synchrotron electrons and GPS satellite clocks both live outside that fine print, and Unit 4's relativity is the reading of it. A third: the practical investigation of motor torque — varying the current, the field and the number of loops — is a dress rehearsal for the student-designed investigation that ends Unit 4.
ai.35: Connects The accelerator endpoint of this story is examined in Pass 3. Induction, electromagnetic waves and relativity belong to Area of Study 3 and Unit 4 — signposted now so that none of them arrives as a stranger.
ai.36: You have now read the whole Area of Study once. Everything from here is the same story at higher resolution, and the book is built so you always know which resolution you are reading.
ai.37: Pass 2 is the core, in five chapters meant to be read the night before your class meets each topic: the field idea; gravity and orbits; electric fields; magnetic fields; motors and accelerators. There you will find every examinable equation with its formula-sheet status, the standard situations, and quick checks to confirm each idea landed.
ai.38: Pass 3 is for exam season. It walks the sixteen key-knowledge dot points in study-design order — the order the exam is written from — with the subtleties, the classic traps, the assessors' advice and exam-style questions with full solutions. Between passes, depth links like the row below carry you up and down, and this page stays one click away for whenever the detail starts to obscure the shape.
ai.39: Connects Begin Pass 2 · The field idea whenever you are ready, and bookmark Pass 3 · Fields and interactions for when the exam is real.

socratic.0:
Socrates. Let us begin the whole of this Area of Study with a single question, and survey rather than solve. Tell me: when one object moves another, what passes between them?
Student. Usually a push or a pull where they touch — a hand on a door, a bat on a ball.
Socrates. And if I told you that the next several months of your physics turn on the cases where nothing touches at all — how an object moves another without contact — would you say we had a small puzzle or a large one?
Student. A large one, I suspect, if it carries a whole year.
socratic.1:
Socrates. Then take the plainest case. You release your phone in mid-air. What reaches up to seize it?
Student. Nothing I can see — no string, no hand, not even a breath of air.
Socrates. And yet?
Student. And yet the instant I let go, it accelerates toward the floor — at 9.8 m s⁻², every single time, whatever the phone.
socratic.2:
Socrates. Now hold three observations together. A compass needle, sealed in its plastic case, swings to the north anywhere in Victoria. A balloon rubbed on your jumper lifts hair toward it from a handsbreadth away. And your falling phone. What do the three share?
Student. In each one, motion begins with nothing touching the thing that moves. The same puzzle, three times over.
socratic.3:
Socrates. You are in distinguished company in being troubled by it. Newton wrote the law of gravitation that still guides spacecraft — would you guess he was content with it?
Student. I'd assume so, since it works so well.
Socrates. Privately he thought the idea of bodies acting on one another across empty space an absurdity, and refused to say how it happened. The official title of this Area of Study is the very question — How do things move without contact? — and everything we do is the modern answer.
Student. So we are finishing an argument Newton wouldn't even start.
socratic.4:
Socrates. Just so. And the answer, when it comes, is remarkably economical. How many ideas would you spend on a year of physics?
Student. As few as I could get away with.
Socrates. Then you will be pleased: the answer is one idea — the field — together with a year of its consequences. We survey the whole thing now, end to end, so that when the detail arrives in Pass 2 it lands in country you already know.
socratic.5:
Connects Socrates. You have in fact met a field strength already. What unit hides behind multiplying 9.8 by a mass?
Student. N kg⁻¹ — newtons of force for every kilogram placed at that point. We used it in Year 11 without naming it.
Socrates. And Pass 2 chapter 4 sets that very unit at the centre of Unit 3.
socratic.6:
Socrates. The answer arrived from an unlikely man. Picture a bookbinder's apprentice, teaching himself science between jobs, watching iron filings settle into arcs around a magnet through the 1830s. Most people saw a pretty pattern. What more might one claim?
Student. That the pattern points at something — that the magnet has changed the space itself.
Socrates. Exactly Faraday's leap. He held that the magnet alters the region around it, and that this altered condition of space — which he called a field — is real, and is there whether or not anything sits in it to be pushed.
socratic.7:
Socrates. The modern definition keeps his idea and sharpens it. Could you frame a field as a kind of value spread over space?
Student. A physical quantity that has a value at every point in space — that's the shape of it.
Socrates. And must that value be elaborate? Consider a sports analyst's heat map of the MCG, recording how often a player stood on each patch of grass.
Student. That's just one number at each point. So that already counts as a field — the simplest kind.
socratic.8:
Socrates. Our fields carry something richer at each point. If a number won't do, what would you place there to capture a force?
Student. An arrow — it has a direction as well as a size.
Socrates. Then say what each part of the arrow tells you.
Student. Its direction is the direction of the force on a suitable test object placed there, and its length is the strength of that force.
Socrates. And a field diagram, with all its lines and arrowheads?
Student. A map of those arrows — direction running along each line, and strength shown by how tightly the lines are packed.
socratic.9:
Socrates. One drawing rule looks like mere tidiness but isn't. Suppose two field lines crossed. What would the field be doing at the crossing point?
Student. Pointing two ways at once — so the force there would have two directions.
Socrates. And can a force act in two directions at once?
Student. No. So field lines can never cross. The rule is forced on us.
socratic.10:
Connects Socrates. You drew force diagrams in Unit 2 — arrows on a single object. How is a field diagram its extension?
Student. Instead of drawing the arrows acting on one object, the arrow is fixed in advance for every point in space.
Socrates. The precise definition, the test objects and the line rules open the core at What is a field?
socratic.11:
Socrates. Unit 3 hands you three such maps. Let me give you each source and ask what it acts on. The gravitational field springs from mass — and acts on?
Student. On mass.
Socrates. The electric field springs from charge, and acts on?
Student. On charge.
Socrates. And the magnetic field springs from moving charge — a current in a wire, or the orbiting and spinning electrons inside a bar magnet. What does it act on?
Student. On moving charge, to match. Figure 1.1 sets all three side by side — and they rhyme.
Socrates. One grammar, then, in three dialects.
socratic.12:
Socrates. But the differences carry the real physics. Is there such a thing as negative mass?
Student. No — mass comes in one kind only.
Socrates. Then what can gravity do, and never do?
Student. It can only attract, never repel — so every gravitational field line points inward. In the language of Figure 1.1, a mass is purely a sink: lines flow in and never out.
socratic.13:
Socrates. Now charge. How many signs does it come in?
Student. Two — positive and negative.
Socrates. So draw the lines for me.
Student. They flow out of positive charges, which are sources, and into negative charges, which are sinks. And either kind can sit alone — an isolated charge is a monopole.
Socrates. And with two signs available, what becomes possible that gravity forbids?
Student. Repulsion as well as attraction — electric forces can do both.
socratic.14:
Socrates. Magnetism keeps the strangest rule. Try to obtain a lone north pole: take a bar magnet and cut it in half. What do you hold?
Student. Two complete magnets, each with its own north and south.
Socrates. Cut again, and again, down to subatomic particles?
Student. Still no lone pole has ever turned up. So magnetic sources are dipoles only — a source and a sink locked together.
Socrates. Which is why, in Figure 1.1, the magnet's lines leave the north face and loop right around to the south.
socratic.15:
Socrates. Set the three side by side. Gravity?
Student. Monopoles of one sign.
Socrates. Electricity?
Student. Monopoles of both signs.
Socrates. Magnetism?
Student. Dipoles only. So the three maps aren't three things to memorise — they're one idea with three settings.
Socrates. And Simulation 1.1 lets you test all three for yourself.
socratic.16:
Connects Socrates. This contrast hardens into an examinable table — sources, directions, shapes, attraction and repulsion, monopoles and dipoles. Where is it built?
Student. In Comparing the three fields — and the exam harvests it dot point by dot point in Pass 3.
socratic.17:
Socrates. That comparison is the first of four threads we'll follow all year. Here is the second's seed. When you lift your schoolbag from the floor to the desk, what are you working against?
Student. The gravitational field.
Socrates. And does that energy you spent simply vanish?
Student. No — it's stored in the bag's raised position. The moment it slides off the desk, it comes back as kinetic energy, in full.
socratic.18:
Socrates. Does any field here behave differently?
Student. No — they all do this. Pull a positive and a negative charge apart and you store energy in their separation; let them go and the energy returns as motion.
Socrates. So what is position in a field, in one phrase?
Student. Stored energy — potential energy. The whole topic is double-entry bookkeeping: energy out of one account, into another, the total never changing.
socratic.19:
Socrates. Mind the unit of account, for it differs by field. Gravitational stores are tallied per what?
Student. Per kilogram of mass.
Socrates. And electrical stores?
Student. Per coulomb of charge — energy per coulomb.
Socrates. A name you've used since Unit 1 hides in that phrase. Which?
Student. Voltage. And W = qV not on sheet says exactly that: the energy transformed when a charge q moves through a potential difference V.
Socrates. The formula sheet folds the same statement into ½mv² = qV — though we calculate nothing yet. Read it as what?
Student. As Unit 1's definition of voltage, run in reverse.
socratic.20:
Socrates. And when a field is not uniform, so the force keeps changing? Then a single multiplication won't tally the store. How did you read such a thing for a spring in Units 1 and 2?
Student. As the area under a force–distance graph.
Socrates. The very same method serves here. One ledger, then —
Student. — just new types of deposit.
socratic.21:
Connects Socrates. Where is each store banked in Pass 2?
Student. Gravitational storage in Gravity and orbits, and electrical storage in Electric fields.
Socrates. And the same ledger reappears — for projectiles and collisions in Area of Study 1, and in Unit 4 the photoelectric effect uses voltage as a measuring instrument for single electrons.
socratic.22:
Socrates. Thread two is not an equation but a shape. Stand Newton's cannon on a mountaintop above the air and fire it horizontally. A slow ball?
Student. Arcs to the ground nearby.
Socrates. A faster one?
Student. Lands further off.
Socrates. And at about 7.9 km s⁻¹, something remarkable. What?
Student. The ball falls toward Earth exactly as fast as Earth's surface curves away beneath it. It's falling continuously and missing continuously.
Socrates. Which is to say?
Student. That's all an orbit is.
socratic.23:
Socrates. Then what is the Moon?
Student. Newton's cannonball, fired long ago. Each second it falls about 1.4 mm toward Earth — how far its curved path drops below the straight line it would otherwise take.
Socrates. And why does that fall never bring it down?
Student. In the same second its sideways velocity carries it far enough that the drop never closes the gap.
Socrates. So what does a satellite need to stay up — an engine?
Student. No engine at all. Only sideways speed and one inward force: gravity.
socratic.24:
Socrates. This unseats a popular belief. People say there's no gravity in space — is there none at the International Space Station?
Student. There must be plenty, or it wouldn't orbit.
Socrates. At that altitude the field strength is still about 8.7 N kg⁻¹ — near 90 per cent of its surface value. So why do astronauts float?
Student. Because the station and everyone in it are falling together. No surface pushes up on anyone — and the normal force is what we feel as weight.
Socrates. So we name it carefully — apparent weightlessness. Why does "apparent" earn its place?
Student. Because the weight is still there; only the sensation of it is gone.
socratic.25:
Socrates. Now watch the same mathematics wear a different costume. A charged particle crosses a magnetic field and feels a force, named by F = qvB on formula sheet. Which way does that force point?
Student. At right angles to the particle's velocity — always.
Socrates. And a sideways force of constant size — what does it do to the path?
Student. It changes the direction but never the speed, so the particle is steered into a circle — just like the satellite.
Socrates. So whether gravity holds a satellite or a magnetic field bends an electron in a synchrotron ring?
Student. It's one piece of circular-motion mathematics in two forms — constant speed, never constant velocity, because the direction never stops changing.
socratic.26:
Connects Socrates. Where are these two built in full?
Student. Orbits in Satellites, and circling charges in Circular paths.
Socrates. And Area of Study 1 supplies the circular-motion rule both obey — so that spotting which force plays the inward, centripetal role collapses many of the year's hardest problems into one.
socratic.27:
Socrates. Thread three turns from explaining nature to building things. Start from the central fact: lay a current-carrying wire across a magnetic field. What happens?
Student. It feels a force — named by F = nIlB on formula sheet.
Socrates. And if you reverse the current?
Student. The force reverses with it.
socratic.28:
Socrates. Bend that wire into a rectangular coil on an axle between two magnet poles. Current runs up one side and down the other — so what do the two sides feel?
Student. Equal forces in opposite directions — a turning pair. The coil rotates.
Socrates. Let it turn half a way round, though. What goes wrong?
Student. The same pair of forces would now turn it back the other way. It would just rock, not spin.
socratic.29:
Socrates. So we need a trick that flips the current at exactly the right moment. Imagine a copper ring cut in two that reverses the current through the coil every half-turn. What does that buy us?
Student. The turning effect always acts the same way — that's the split-ring commutator. And the whole thing is a DC motor, the machine inside every drill, fan and electric vehicle.
Socrates. We'll draw it in detail later. Now run it backwards — spin the coil by wind, water or steam. What flows?
Student. A current is driven through the wire instead. That machine is a generator — the whole subject of Area of Study 3.
socratic.30:
Socrates. The other great field machine is the particle accelerator, and its whole operation is two lines of this Area of Study. First, what does a uniform electric field do to a charge along a straight line?
Student. It adds kinetic energy.
Socrates. And a uniform magnetic field?
Student. Bends the charge's path without changing its speed.
Socrates. Chain the two together, as the Australian Synchrotron in Clayton does, and how fast do the electrons go?
Student. More than 99.99 per cent of the speed of light.
socratic.31:
Connects Socrates. The motor — with the labelled split-ring commutator diagram examiners ask about almost every year — is built where?
Student. In Motors & accelerators.
Socrates. And Area of Study 3 runs the very same hardware in reverse to supply the state's electricity.
socratic.32:
Socrates. The last thread reaches past this Area of Study. One dot point asks a small sorting task: label every field as static or changing, and as uniform or non-uniform. Does that sound important to you?
Student. Honestly, it sounds like bookkeeping — a minor classification.
Socrates. Hold that judgement. It is the most consequential classification of the year.
socratic.33:
Socrates. Why so? Begin with a changing magnetic field near a conductor. What does it do?
Student. It drives a current in that conductor.
Socrates. That single fact is Faraday's induction — and how nearly every power station on Earth makes electricity. There is Area of Study 3 in one sentence. Now let Unit 4 extend it: a changing electric field creates a changing magnetic field, which recreates the electric field —
Student. — so the pair keeps regenerating itself, and detaches from charges and wires altogether and travels through empty space.
Socrates. And that travelling ripple of fields is?
Student. Light. So the field, brought in just to keep books on forces, turns out to be a thing in its own right.
socratic.34:
Socrates. Two loose threads remain. The study design warns that the circular-path rule for charges holds only while the speed v is much less than c. Who breaks that condition?
Student. Synchrotron electrons and GPS satellite clocks both — they sit outside it. Unit 4's relativity must deal with them.
Socrates. And the third thread, nearer to hand?
Student. The practical investigation of motor torque — varying the current, the field and the number of loops — a warm-up for the student-designed investigation that ends Unit 4.
socratic.35:
Connects Socrates. Where is the accelerator endpoint examined?
Student. In Pass 3. And induction, electromagnetic waves and relativity belong to Area of Study 3 and Unit 4 — signposted now so none of them arrives a stranger.
socratic.36:
Socrates. We have now walked the whole Area of Study once. Is there anything left to learn, or only the same things more closely?
Student. The same content at higher resolution, I'd say.
Socrates. Precisely — and the book is built so the current resolution is always plain.
socratic.37:
Socrates. Pass 2 is the core — five chapters meant for the night before each topic is taught. Can you name them?
Student. The field idea; gravity and orbits; electric fields; magnetic fields; motors and accelerators.
Socrates. And what does it carry?
Student. Every examinable equation with its formula-sheet status, the standard situations, and quick checks to confirm each idea has landed.
socratic.38:
Socrates. And Pass 3 — when is it for?
Student. Exam season. It walks the sixteen key-knowledge dot points in study-design order — the order the exam is written from — with the subtleties, the classic traps, the assessors' advice, and exam-style questions with full solutions.

Connects Socrates. So, when you are ready, where do you go?
Student. Begin Pass 2 · The field idea — and bookmark Pass 3 · Fields and interactions for the exam period.

curie.6: The answer arrived from an unlikely direction. Michael Faraday — a bookbinder’s apprentice who taught himself science between jobs — spent the 1830s observing iron filings arrange themselves into arcs around magnets. Where others saw only a pattern, Faraday observed something more: the magnet alters the space around it, and that altered condition of space — the region he termed a field — is real, present whether or not anything is there to be acted on.
curie.7: The modern definition retains his idea and refines it: a field is a physical quantity that has a value at every point in space. Some fields hold a single number at each point — a sports analyst’s heat map, recording how often a player occupied each patch of the MCG, is exactly this kind of field.
curie.8: The fields in this Area of Study hold something richer at every point: an arrow. The arrow’s direction indicates the direction of the force exerted on a suitable test object placed there, and its length indicates the strength of that force. A field diagram, with its lines and arrowheads, is a map of those arrows — direction along the line, strength shown by how closely the lines are spaced.
curie.9: One drawing rule carries real significance: field lines never cross. If two lines met, the field at that point would have two directions at once, and no force can act in two directions at once.
curie.10: Connects A field diagram is the Unit 2 force diagram extended: instead of drawing the arrows acting on one object, the arrow is fixed in advance for every point in space. The precise definition, test objects and field-line rules open the core at What is a field?
curie.11: Unit 3 introduces three of these maps. The gravitational field is sourced by mass and acts on mass. The electric field is sourced by charge and acts on charge. The magnetic field is sourced by moving charge — a current in a wire, or the orbiting and spinning electrons inside a bar magnet — and acts on moving charge. Figure 1.1 draws the three side by side, and the family resemblance is worth noting: one grammar, three dialects.
curie.12: The differences carry the physics. Mass occurs in one kind only — there is no negative mass — so gravity only ever attracts, and every gravitational field line points inward. In the terms of Figure 1.1, a mass is purely a sink: lines flow in, never out.
curie.13: Electric charge occurs in two signs. Field lines flow out of positive charges (sources) and into negative charges (sinks), and either kind can exist on its own — an isolated charge is called a monopole. Two signs means electric forces can attract or repel.
curie.14: Magnetism follows the most unusual rule of the three. No isolated north or south pole has ever been found: a bar magnet cut in half yields two complete magnets, each with both poles, down to subatomic particles. Magnetic sources are dipoles only — a source and a sink permanently paired — which is why the magnet’s lines in Figure 1.1 leave the north face and loop around to the south.
curie.15: This three-way contrast — gravity: monopoles of one sign; electricity: monopoles of both signs; magnetism: dipoles only — turns the three maps from three separate things to memorise into one idea with three settings. Simulation 1.1 allows all three to be tested.
curie.16: Connects This comparison becomes an examinable table — sources, directions, shapes, attraction and repulsion, monopoles and dipoles — in Comparing the three fields, and the exam harvests it dot point by dot point in Pass 3.
curie.17: This is the first of four threads carried through the year. Lifting a schoolbag from the floor to the desk does work against the gravitational field. That energy does not vanish: it is stored in the bag’s raised position, and the moment the bag slides off the desk it is returned as kinetic energy, in full.
curie.18: Every field in this Area of Study operates the same way. Separating a positive and a negative charge stores energy in their separation; releasing them returns that energy to motion. Position in a field is stored energy — potential energy — and the whole topic is double-entry bookkeeping: energy out of one account, into another, total unchanged.
curie.19: The accounting unit is the detail to note. Gravitational stores are tallied per kilogram of mass; electrical stores are tallied per coulomb of charge — and "energy per coulomb" is a quantity used since Unit 1 under its everyday name, voltage. One formula, W = qV not on sheet, states exactly this: the energy transformed when a charge q moves through a potential difference V — the formula sheet carries the same statement folded into ½mv² = qV. Nothing is to be calculated yet; this is Unit 1’s definition of voltage read in reverse.
curie.20: When a field is not uniform, the store is read graphically, as the area under a force–distance graph — the same method Units 1&2 used for springs. One ledger, new deposit types.
curie.21: Connects Gravitational storage is banked in Gravity and orbits and electrical storage in Electric fields. Area of Study 1 runs the identical ledger for projectiles and collisions, and in Unit 4 the photoelectric effect will use voltage as a measuring instrument for single electrons.
curie.22: Thread two is a shape. Newton imagined a cannon on a mountaintop above the atmosphere, fired horizontally. A slow cannonball arcs to the ground nearby; a faster one lands further away; and at about 7.9 km s⁻¹ a notable result occurs — the ball falls toward Earth exactly as fast as Earth’s surface curves away beneath it. It is falling continuously, and missing continuously: that is all an orbit is.
curie.23: The Moon is Newton’s cannonball, fired long ago. In every second it falls about 1.4 mm toward Earth — the distance its curved path drops below the straight line it would otherwise follow — and in that same second its sideways velocity carries it far enough that the drop never brings it closer. A satellite requires no engine to stay up; it requires sideways speed and a single inward force: gravity.
curie.24: This corrects a common belief about astronauts. At the International Space Station’s altitude the gravitational field strength is still about 8.7 N kg⁻¹ — close to 90 per cent of its surface value — so the statement that there is no gravity in space is incorrect. Astronauts float because the station and everyone inside it are falling together: no surface exerts a normal force on anyone, and the normal force is what is felt as weight. This state is termed apparent weightlessness, and the word "apparent" is significant.
curie.25: The same mathematics appears in a different form. A charged particle moving across a magnetic field experiences a force, named by F = qvB on formula sheet, that points at right angles to the particle’s velocity — always. A sideways force of constant size changes a path’s direction but never its speed, so the particle is steered into a circle, exactly as the satellite is. Whether the inward role is played by gravity on a satellite or by a magnetic field on an electron in a synchrotron ring, it is one piece of circular-motion mathematics in two forms — constant speed, never constant velocity, because the direction never stops changing.
curie.26: Connects Orbits are built properly in Satellites and circling charges in Circular paths. Area of Study 1 supplies the circular-motion rule both obey — identifying which force plays the inward, centripetal role reduces many of the year’s hardest problems to one.
curie.27: Thread three: fields are not only for explaining nature; they can also be used to build. The starting point is the central fact of this Area of Study: a current-carrying wire placed across a magnetic field experiences a force, named by F = nIlB on formula sheet. Reversing the current reverses the force.
curie.28: When the wire is bent into a rectangular coil on an axle between two magnet poles, current travels up one side of the coil and down the other, so the two sides experience equal forces in opposite directions — a turning pair, and the coil rotates. There is one flaw in this design: half a turn later, the same pair of forces would turn it back the other way.
curie.29: The remedy is the split-ring commutator — a copper ring cut in two that reverses the current through the coil every half-turn, so the turning effect always acts in the same direction. This is a DC motor, the machine inside every drill, fan and electric vehicle, and a diagram studied in detail later. Run in reverse — the coil spun by wind, water or steam — a current is driven through the wire instead: that machine is a generator, the whole subject of Area of Study 3.
curie.30: The other major field machine is the particle accelerator, whose operation is a two-line summary of this Area of Study: a uniform electric field adds kinetic energy to a charge along a straight line, and a uniform magnetic field bends the charge’s path without changing its speed. The Australian Synchrotron in Clayton chains the two together, holding electrons at more than 99.99 per cent of the speed of light.
curie.31: Connects The motor — with the labelled split-ring commutator diagram examiners ask about almost every year — is built in Motors & accelerators. Area of Study 3 runs the identical hardware in reverse to supply the state’s electricity.
curie.32: The last thread points beyond this Area of Study. Among its dot points is a small act of sorting: classify every field as static or changing, and as uniform or non-uniform. This appears to be a minor matter; it is the most consequential classification of the year.
curie.33: Changing magnetic fields drive currents in nearby conductors — that single fact, Faraday’s discovery of induction, is how almost every power station on Earth generates electricity, and it is Area of Study 3 in one sentence. Unit 4 then extends the idea further: a changing electric field creates a changing magnetic field, which recreates the electric field, and the self-sustaining pair detaches from charges and wires altogether and travels through empty space. That travelling ripple of fields is light. The field, introduced as a bookkeeping device for forces, proves to be a thing in its own right.
curie.34: A second loose thread is printed in the study design itself: the circular-path rule for charges is guaranteed only while the speed v is much less than c. Synchrotron electrons and GPS satellite clocks both lie outside that condition, and Unit 4’s relativity addresses it. A third: the practical investigation of motor torque — varying the current, the field and the number of loops — is a preliminary to the student-designed investigation that ends Unit 4.
curie.35: Connects The accelerator endpoint of this topic is examined in Pass 3. Induction, electromagnetic waves and relativity belong to Area of Study 3 and Unit 4 — signposted now so that none of them arrives unfamiliar.
curie.36: The whole Area of Study has now been surveyed once. Everything from here is the same content at higher resolution, and the book is structured so the current resolution is always clear.
curie.37: Pass 2 is the core, in five chapters intended to be read the night before each topic is taught: the field idea; gravity and orbits; electric fields; magnetic fields; motors and accelerators. It contains every examinable equation with its formula-sheet status, the standard situations, and quick checks to confirm each idea has been understood.
curie.38: Pass 3 is for exam season. It walks the sixteen key-knowledge dot points in study-design order — the order the exam is written from — with the subtleties, the classic traps, the assessors’ advice and exam-style questions with full solutions. Between passes, depth links like the row below move up and down, and this page remains one click away whenever the detail begins to obscure the overall shape.

levin.3: This puzzle has unsettled the greatest minds. levin.4: And the reply is a single idea, the field, trailing a year of consequences behind it. This overview carries the topic end to end, so that when the detail arrives in Pass 2 it arrives in country you already know.
levin.5: Connects A field strength has been in your hands since Year 11: the 9.8 you multiply by masses carries the unit N kg⁻¹ — newtons of force for every kilogram set at that point. Pass 2 chapter 4 sets that unit at the very heart of Unit 3.
levin.6: The answer came from an unlikely quarter. levin.7: The modern definition keeps his idea and sharpens it: a field is a physical quantity with a value at every point in space. levin.8: The fields in this Area of Study hold something richer at every point: an arrow. levin.9: One drawing rule carries real weight: field lines never cross. If two lines met, the field at that point would point two ways at once — and no force can act in two directions at the same time.

levin.11: Unit 3 hands you three of these maps. The gravitational field is sourced by mass and acts on mass. The electric field is sourced by charge and acts on charge. Figure 1.1 draws the three side by side, and the family resemblance matters: one grammar, three dialects.
levin.12: The differences carry the physics. Mass comes in one kind only — there is no negative mass — so gravity only ever pulls, and every gravitational field line points inward. In the language of Figure 1.1, a mass is purely a sink: lines flow in, never out.
levin.13: Electric charge comes in two signs. Field lines flow out of positive charges (sources) and into negative charges (sinks), and either kind can stand alone — a lone charge is called a monopole. Two signs means electric forces can attract or repel.
levin.14: Magnetism keeps the strangest rule of the three. No lone north or south pole has ever been found: cut a bar magnet in half and you get two whole magnets, each with both poles, all the way down to the subatomic. feynman.4: And the answer is just one idea, the field, plus a year of working out what it implies. This overview walks you through the topic from end to end, so that when the details turn up in Pass 2 you already know your way around the place.
feynman.5: Connects You’ve been using a field strength since Year 11: that number 9.8 you multiply masses by carries the unit N kg⁻¹ — newtons of force for every kilogram you put at that point. Pass 2 chapter 4 puts that unit right at the heart of Unit 3.
feynman.6: The answer came from a surprising place. Michael Faraday — a bookbinder’s apprentice who taught himself science between jobs — spent the 1830s watching iron filings line themselves up into arcs around magnets. Where everyone else saw just a pattern, Faraday saw something more: the magnet changes the space around it, and that altered condition of the space — the region he called a field — is really there, whether or not there’s anything around for it to push on.
feynman.7: The modern definition keeps his idea and sharpens it up: a field is a physical quantity that has a value at every point in space. Some fields carry just one number at each point — a sports analyst’s heat map, showing how often a player stood on each patch of the MCG, is exactly this kind of field.
feynman.8: The fields in this Area of Study carry something fancier at every point: an arrow. Which way the arrow points tells you which way the force pushes on a suitable test object you put there, and how long it is tells you how strong that push is. A field diagram, with all its lines and arrowheads, is just a map of those arrows — direction runs along the line, and strength shows up in how tightly the lines are packed together.
feynman.9: One drawing rule actually matters: field lines never cross. If two lines did meet, the field at that point would be pointing two ways at once — and no force can shove in two directions at the same time.
feynman.10: Connects A field diagram is really just the Unit 2 force diagram stretched out: instead of drawing the arrows on one object, you work the arrow out ahead of time for every point in space. The careful definition, the test objects and the field-line rules all open up the core at What is a field?
feynman.11: Unit 3 hands you three of these maps. The gravitational field comes from mass and acts on mass. The electric field comes from charge and acts on charge. The magnetic field comes from moving charge — a current in a wire, or the orbiting and spinning electrons inside a bar magnet — and it acts on moving charge. Figure 1.1 draws all three side by side, and the family resemblance really matters: one grammar, three dialects.
feynman.12: The differences are where the physics lives. Mass comes in only one kind — there’s no such thing as negative mass — so gravity only ever pulls, and every gravitational field line points inward. In the language of Figure 1.1, a mass is purely a sink: lines flow in, never out.
feynman.13: Electric charge comes in two signs. Field lines flow out of positive charges (sources) and into negative charges (sinks), and either kind can sit there all by itself — a lone charge is called a monopole. Two signs means electric forces can attract or repel.
feynman.14: Magnetism plays by the strangest rule of the three. Nobody has ever found a lone north or south pole: cut a bar magnet in half and you get two complete magnets, each with both poles, and it keeps going right down to subatomic particles. Magnetic sources are dipoles only — a source and a sink locked together forever — which is why the magnet’s lines in Figure 1.1 leave the north face and loop around to the south.
feynman.15: This three-way contrast — gravity: monopoles of one sign; electricity: monopoles of both signs; magnetism: dipoles only — turns the three maps from three separate things to memorise into one idea with three settings. Simulation 1.1 lets you try all three out for yourself.
feynman.16: Connects This comparison turns into an examinable table — sources, directions, shapes, attraction and repulsion, monopoles and dipoles — in Comparing the three fields, and the exam harvests it dot point by dot point in Pass 3.
feynman.17: Here’s the first of four threads we’ll follow all year. Lifting a schoolbag from the floor up to the desk does work against the gravitational field. That energy doesn’t just disappear: it gets stored in the bag’s raised position, and the moment the bag slides off the desk it’s handed straight back as kinetic energy, every bit of it.
feynman.18: Every field in this Area of Study works the same way. Pull a positive and a negative charge apart and you store energy in their separation; let them go and that energy comes back as motion. Position in a field is stored energy — potential energy — and the whole topic is just double-entry bookkeeping: energy out of one account, into another, the total never changing.
feynman.19: The unit you keep the books in is the detail worth noticing. Gravitational stores get tallied per kilogram of mass; electrical stores get tallied per coulomb of charge — and “energy per coulomb” is a quantity you’ve been using since Unit 1 under its everyday name, voltage. One formula, W = qV not on sheet, says exactly this: the energy transformed when a charge q moves through a potential difference V — and the formula sheet carries the same statement folded into ½mv² = qV. Don’t calculate anything yet; this is just Unit 1’s definition of voltage read backwards.
feynman.20: When a field isn’t uniform, you read the store off a graph instead — it’s the area under a force–distance graph, the very same trick Units 1&2 used for springs. One ledger, new kinds of deposit.
feynman.21: Connects Gravitational storage gets banked in Gravity and orbits and electrical storage in Electric fields. Area of Study 1 runs the identical ledger for projectiles and collisions, and in Unit 4 the photoelectric effect will use voltage as a measuring instrument for single electrons.
feynman.22: Thread two is a shape. Newton pictured a cannon on a mountaintop up above the air, fired sideways. A slow cannonball arcs down to the ground nearby; a faster one lands further off; and at about 7.9 km s⁻¹ something remarkable happens — the ball falls toward the Earth at exactly the rate the Earth’s surface curves away beneath it. It’s falling the whole time, and missing the whole time: that is all an orbit is.
feynman.23: The Moon is Newton’s cannonball, fired a long time ago. Every second it falls about 1.4 mm toward Earth — that’s how far its curved path drops below the straight line it would otherwise travel — and in that same second its sideways motion carries it far enough that the drop never gets it any closer. A satellite needs no engine to stay up; it just needs sideways speed and one single inward force: gravity.
feynman.24: This sets straight a thing people get wrong about astronauts. Up at the International Space Station’s altitude the gravitational field strength is still about 8.7 N kg⁻¹ — close to 90 per cent of what it is at the surface — so saying there’s no gravity in space is just wrong. Astronauts float because the station and everyone inside it are all falling together: no surface pushes a normal force on anybody, and the normal force is what you actually feel as weight. We call this state apparent weightlessness, and that word “apparent” is doing real work.
feynman.25: The very same mathematics shows up wearing different clothes. A charged particle moving across a magnetic field feels a force, written F = qvB on formula sheet, that points at right angles to the particle’s velocity — always. A sideways force of fixed size changes which way a path points but never how fast, so the particle gets steered around into a circle, exactly the way the satellite does. Whether the inward role is played by gravity on a satellite or by a magnetic field on an electron in a synchrotron ring, it’s one piece of circular-motion mathematics in two outfits — constant speed, never constant velocity, because the direction never stops changing.
feynman.26: Connects Orbits get built properly in Satellites and circling charges in Circular paths. Area of Study 1 supplies the circular-motion rule both obey — figuring out which force is playing the inward, centripetal role boils a lot of the year’s hardest problems down to one.
feynman.27: Thread three: fields aren’t only for explaining nature — you can also use them to build things. The starting point is the central fact of this whole Area of Study: a current-carrying wire sitting across a magnetic field feels a force, written F = nIlB on formula sheet. Reverse the current and you reverse the force.
feynman.28: Bend the wire into a rectangular coil on an axle between two magnet poles, and the current runs up one side of the coil and down the other, so the two sides feel equal forces in opposite directions — a turning pair, and the coil spins. There’s one snag in this design: half a turn later, that same pair of forces would turn it right back the other way.
feynman.29: The fix is the split-ring commutator — a copper ring cut in two that flips the current through the coil every half-turn, so the turning effect always pushes the same way. This is a DC motor, the machine inside every drill, fan and electric vehicle, and we’ll study a diagram of it in detail later. Run it backwards — spin the coil with wind, water or steam — and instead it drives a current through the wire: that machine is a generator, the whole subject of Area of Study 3.
feynman.30: The other big field machine is the particle accelerator, and how it works is a two-line summary of this Area of Study: a uniform electric field adds kinetic energy to a charge along a straight line, and a uniform magnetic field bends the charge’s path without changing its speed. The Australian Synchrotron in Clayton chains the two together, holding electrons at more than 99.99 per cent of the speed of light.
feynman.31: Connects The motor — with the labelled split-ring commutator diagram examiners ask about almost every year — gets built in Motors & accelerators. Area of Study 3 runs the identical hardware in reverse to supply the state’s electricity.
feynman.32: The last thread points beyond this Area of Study. Among its dot points is one small sorting job: label every field as static or changing, and as uniform or non-uniform. It looks like a minor thing; it’s actually the most consequential classification of the whole year.
feynman.33: Changing magnetic fields drive currents in nearby conductors — that one fact, Faraday’s discovery of induction, is how nearly every power station on Earth makes electricity, and it’s Area of Study 3 in a single sentence. Then Unit 4 takes the idea even further: a changing electric field makes a changing magnetic field, which remakes the electric field, and the self-sustaining pair breaks free of charges and wires altogether and goes travelling through empty space. That travelling ripple of fields is light. The field, which we brought in as a bookkeeping trick for forces, turns out to be a thing in its own right.
feynman.34: There’s a second loose thread printed right into the study design: the circular-path rule for charges only holds for sure while the speed v is much less than c. Synchrotron electrons and GPS satellite clocks both sit outside that condition, and Unit 4’s relativity deals with it. And a third: the practical investigation of motor torque — tweaking the current, the field and the number of loops — is a warm-up for the student-designed investigation that closes out Unit 4.
feynman.35: Connects The accelerator endpoint of this topic is examined in Pass 3. Induction, electromagnetic waves and relativity belong to Area of Study 3 and Unit 4 — we’re signposting them now so none of them turns up as a stranger.
feynman.36: So that’s the whole Area of Study surveyed once. Everything from here on is the same content at higher resolution, and the book is built so you always know which resolution you’re looking at.
feynman.37: Pass 2 is the core, in five chapters meant to be read the night before each topic gets taught: the field idea; gravity and orbits; electric fields; magnetic fields; motors and accelerators. It carries every examinable equation with its formula-sheet status, the standard situations, and quick checks so you can confirm each idea has actually landed.
feynman.38: Pass 3 is for exam season. It walks the sixteen key-knowledge dot points in study-design order — the order the exam is written from — with the subtleties, the classic traps, the assessors’ advice and exam-style questions with full solutions. Between passes, depth links like the row below move up and down, and this page stays one click away whenever the detail starts to hide the overall shape.
feynman.39: Connects Start Pass 2 · The field idea when you’re ready, and bookmark Pass 3 · Fields and interactions for the exam period.

sagan.0: The whole of Unit 3 Area of Study 2 — and a great part of the year still to come — unfolds from a single, ancient question: how can one object reach across emptiness and move another it never touches? This overview holds up the whole picture at once. None of it is to be solved here; for now we only stand back and survey the landscape.
sagan.1: Let go of a phone and it falls. Nothing visible reaches up to seize it — no string, no hand, no breath of air — and yet in the very instant of release something takes hold of it and draws it toward the floor, accelerating it at 9.8 m s⁻¹, every time, without exception.
sagan.2: A compass needle, sealed away inside its plastic case, swings faithfully to the north anywhere in Victoria. A balloon rubbed against a jumper reaches out and lifts hair from a handsbreadth away. These three small wonders share one deep puzzle: in each, motion is born where nothing has touched anything at all.
sagan.3: This puzzle has haunted some of the greatest minds. Newton wrote down the law of gravitation that guides our spacecraft to this day, and yet he privately confessed that the idea of bodies acting upon one another across empty space struck him as an absurdity, and he refused to say how it could happen. The whole of this Area of Study — its official title is How do things move without contact? — is our long modern answer to a question that once seemed beyond answering.
sagan.4: And the answer is a single luminous idea — the field — trailing a year of consequences behind it. This overview carries you across the topic from one shore to the other, so that when the detail arrives in Pass 2, it arrives in a country you have already walked once before.
sagan.5: Connects You have already held a field strength in your hand since Year 11: the familiar 9.8, multiplied by masses, carries the unit N kg⁻¹ — newtons of force for every kilogram you place at that point in space. Pass 2 chapter 4 takes that quiet unit and sets it at the very centre of Unit 3.
sagan.6: The answer came from the most unlikely quarter. Michael Faraday — a bookbinder’s apprentice who taught himself science in the hours between jobs — spent the 1830s watching iron filings arrange themselves into delicate arcs around a magnet. Where others saw merely a pretty pattern, Faraday glimpsed something far stranger and far greater: the magnet alters the very space around it, and that altered condition of space — the region he named a field — is no fiction but a real thing, present whether or not anything happens to be there to feel it.
sagan.7: The modern definition keeps Faraday’s vision and sharpens it: a field is a physical quantity that has a value at every point in space. Some fields carry a single number at each point — a sports analyst’s heat map, recording how often a player lingered on each patch of the MCG, is exactly such a field.
sagan.8: The fields of this Area of Study carry something richer at every point: an arrow. The arrow’s direction tells you which way the force would push a suitable test object set down there, and its length tells you how strongly. A field diagram, with its lines and arrowheads, is nothing less than a map of those arrows — direction running along the line, strength written in how closely the lines crowd together.
sagan.9: One drawing rule carries a deep truth: field lines never cross. For if two lines were to meet, the field at that point would somehow point in two directions at once — and no force in the universe can pull in two directions at the same instant.
sagan.10: Connects A field diagram is the Unit 2 force diagram set free: instead of drawing the arrows acting on one chosen object, the arrow is settled in advance for every point in all of space. The precise definition, the test objects and the field-line rules open the core at What is a field?
sagan.11: Unit 3 brings us three such maps. The gravitational field is born of mass and acts on mass. The electric field is born of charge and acts on charge. The magnetic field is born of moving charge — a current in a wire, or the orbiting and spinning electrons hidden inside a bar magnet — and acts on moving charge. Figure 1.1 sets the three side by side, and their family resemblance is no accident: one grammar, spoken in three dialects.
sagan.12: It is in the differences that the physics truly lives. Mass comes in one kind only — there is no such thing as negative mass — so gravity can only ever draw things together, and every gravitational field line points inward. In the language of Figure 1.1, a mass is purely a sink: lines flow in, and never out.
sagan.13: Electric charge comes in two signs. Field lines stream out of positive charges (the sources) and pour into negative charges (the sinks), and either kind may stand alone — an isolated charge is called a monopole. Two signs mean that electric forces can either attract or repel.
sagan.14: Magnetism obeys the strangest rule of the three. No isolated north or south pole has ever been found anywhere: cut a bar magnet in half and you do not get a lone north and a lone south — you get two whole magnets, each with both poles, and so on down to the very subatomic particles. Magnetic sources are dipoles and dipoles alone — a source and a sink forever bound together — which is why the magnet’s lines in Figure 1.1 leave the north face and arc gracefully around to the south.
sagan.15: This three-way contrast — gravity: monopoles of one sign; electricity: monopoles of both signs; magnetism: dipoles only — transforms the three maps from three separate things to be memorised into one single idea wearing three settings. Simulation 1.1 lets you put all three to the test.
sagan.16: Connects This comparison hardens into an examinable table — sources, directions, shapes, attraction and repulsion, monopoles and dipoles — in Comparing the three fields, and the exam harvests it dot point by dot point in Pass 3.
sagan.17: This is the first of four threads we follow through the whole year. To lift a schoolbag from the floor to the desk is to do work against the gravitational field. That energy does not simply vanish into nothing: it is stored in the bag’s raised position, and the very moment the bag slides off the desk, it is handed back, in full, as kinetic energy.
sagan.18: Every field in this Area of Study keeps faith with the same law. Drag a positive and a negative charge apart and you store energy in their separation; let them go and that energy returns as motion. Position within a field is stored energy — potential energy — and the whole topic is double-entry bookkeeping: energy drawn out of one account, paid into another, the total forever unchanged.
sagan.19: It is the unit of account that rewards close attention. Gravitational stores are tallied per kilogram of mass; electrical stores are tallied per coulomb of charge — and “energy per coulomb” is a quantity you have used since Unit 1 under its everyday name, voltage. One formula, W = qV not on sheet, says exactly this: the energy transformed when a charge q moves through a potential difference V — and the formula sheet carries the very same truth folded into ½mv² = qV. Nothing is to be calculated yet; this is simply Unit 1’s definition of voltage, read in reverse.
sagan.20: When a field is not uniform, the store is read off graphically, as the area beneath a force–distance graph — the very same method Units 1&2 used for springs. One ledger, only new kinds of deposit.

sagan.25: The same mathematics returns to us wearing a different face. A charged particle moving across a magnetic field feels a force, named by F = qvB on formula sheet, that points at right angles to the particle’s velocity — always, without fail. Unit 4 then carries the idea further still: a changing electric field gives birth to a changing magnetic field, which gives birth again to the electric field, and the self-sustaining pair tears free of all charges and wires and races out through empty space. That travelling ripple of fields is light. The field, first introduced as a humble bookkeeping device for forces, turns out to be a thing in its own right, alive in the void.

tyson.22: Thread two is a shape. Newton pictured a cannon on a mountaintop above the atmosphere, fired flat. A slow cannonball arcs to the ground nearby; a faster one lands further out; and at around 7.9 km s⁻¹ something remarkable happens — the ball falls toward Earth exactly as fast as Earth’s surface curves away beneath it. tyson.23: The Moon is Newton’s cannonball, fired a long time ago. tyson.25: The same mathematics shows up wearing a different costume. cox.0: Everything in Unit 3 Area of Study 2 — and a good deal of the rest of the year — grows out of one simple, almost childlike question: how can one object move another without ever touching it? This overview holds the whole picture up to the light at once. We’re not here to solve any of it yet; we’re just here to look.
cox.1: Let go of your phone and it falls. Nothing visible reaches up to take it — no string, no hand, not even a breath of air — and yet the very instant you release it, something pulls it toward the floor, accelerating it at 9.8 m s⁻², every single time.
cox.2: A compass needle, sealed away inside its little plastic case, quietly swings to the north anywhere in Victoria. A balloon rubbed on a jumper reaches out and lifts your hair from a handsbreadth away. Three small, everyday observations, and they all share the same lovely puzzle: in each one, motion appears with no contact at all.
cox.3: This puzzle has troubled some of the greatest minds we have. Newton wrote down the law of gravitation that still guides our spacecraft today — and yet, privately, he thought the idea of bodies reaching across empty space to act on one another was an absurdity, and he refused to say how it might happen. This whole Area of Study — its official title is How do things move without contact? — is our modern answer to the question that defeated him.
cox.4: And the answer turns out to be a single idea, the field, together with a whole year of its consequences. This overview walks the topic from one end to the other, so that when the detail arrives in Pass 2, it arrives in a landscape you already recognise.
cox.5: Connects You’ve been using a field strength since Year 11 without naming it: the 9.8 you multiply masses by carries the unit N kg⁻¹ — newtons of force for every kilogram you place at that point. Pass 2 chapter 4 takes that quiet little unit and puts it at the very centre of Unit 3.
cox.6: The answer came from a wonderfully unlikely direction. Michael Faraday — a bookbinder’s apprentice who taught himself science in the gaps between jobs — spent the 1830s watching iron filings settle themselves into arcs around magnets. Where others saw only a pretty pattern, Faraday saw something far bolder: the magnet changes the space around it, and that altered condition of space — the region he called a field — is real, there whether or not anything is present to feel it.
cox.7: The modern definition keeps his idea and sharpens it: a field is a physical quantity that has a value at every point in space. Some fields hold just a single number at each point — a sports analyst’s heat map, recording how often a player stood on each patch of the MCG, is exactly this kind of field.
cox.8: But the fields in this Area of Study hold something richer at every point: an arrow. The arrow’s direction tells you which way the force pushes a suitable test object placed there, and its length tells you how strong that force is. A field diagram, with all its lines and arrowheads, is simply a map of those arrows — direction running along the line, strength shown by how tightly the lines are packed together.
cox.9: And there’s one drawing rule that carries real meaning: field lines never cross. If two lines ever met, the field at that point would be pointing in two directions at once — and no force can pull in two directions at the same time.
cox.10: Connects A field diagram is really the Unit 2 force diagram, stretched out everywhere: instead of drawing the arrows acting on one object, we settle the arrow in advance for every point in space. The precise definition, the test objects and the field-line rules open the core at What is a field?
cox.11: Unit 3 hands us three of these maps. The gravitational field is sourced by mass and acts on mass. The electric field is sourced by charge and acts on charge. The magnetic field is sourced by moving charge — a current in a wire, or the orbiting and spinning electrons inside a bar magnet — and it acts on moving charge. Figure 1.1 draws the three of them side by side, and the family resemblance is no accident: one grammar, three dialects.
cox.12: And it’s the differences that carry the real physics. Mass comes in one kind only — there is no such thing as negative mass — so gravity can only ever attract, and every gravitational field line points inward. In the language of Figure 1.1, a mass is purely a sink: the lines flow in, and never out.
cox.13: Electric charge, by contrast, comes in two signs. Field lines flow out of positive charges (sources) and into negative charges (sinks), and either kind can sit there quite alone — an isolated charge is called a monopole. Two signs means electric forces can attract or repel.
cox.14: Magnetism follows the strangest rule of the three. No isolated north or south pole has ever been found: cut a bar magnet in half and you get two complete magnets, each with both poles, and you can keep going right down to subatomic particles. Magnetic sources are dipoles only — a source and a sink locked together for good — which is why the magnet’s lines in Figure 1.1 leave the north face and loop all the way round to the south.
cox.15: This three-way contrast — gravity: monopoles of one sign; electricity: monopoles of both signs; magnetism: dipoles only — turns the three maps from three separate things you must memorise into one idea with three settings. Simulation 1.1 lets you try all three for yourself.
cox.16: Connects This comparison becomes an examinable table — sources, directions, shapes, attraction and repulsion, monopoles and dipoles — in Comparing the three fields, and the exam harvests it dot point by dot point in Pass 3.
