hawking.0: All of Unit 3 Area of Study 2 — and much of the year that follows — grows from a single question: how one object can move another without touching it. This overview shows you the whole picture. Solve none of it yet. Just survey it.
hawking.1: A phone is released. It falls. Nothing visible reaches up to take it — no string, no hand, no current of air — yet the instant it lets go, something accelerates it toward the floor at 9.8 m s⁻². Every time.
hawking.2: A compass needle, sealed in plastic, swings to the north anywhere in Victoria. A balloon rubbed on a jumper pulls hair across a handsbreadth of empty space. Three small observations, one puzzle: in each, motion begins with no contact at all.
hawking.3: The puzzle has troubled great minds. Newton wrote the law of gravitation that still steers spacecraft, yet he privately thought the idea of bodies acting on one another across empty space an absurdity, and refused to say how it happened. This entire Area of Study — its official title is How do things move without contact? — is the modern answer.
hawking.4: The answer is one idea, the field, and a year of its consequences. This overview runs the topic from end to end, so that when the detail arrives in Pass 2, it arrives in a landscape you already know.
hawking.5: Connects You have used a field strength since Year 11. The value 9.8, multiplied by masses, carries the unit N kg⁻¹ — newtons of force for every kilogram placed at that point. Pass 2 chapter 4 puts that unit at the centre of Unit 3.
hawking.6: The answer came from an unlikely direction. Michael Faraday — a bookbinder’s apprentice who taught himself science between jobs — spent the 1830s watching iron filings line up into arcs around magnets. Others saw a pattern. Faraday saw more: the magnet changes the space around it, and that changed condition of space — the region he called a field — is real. It is there whether or not anything is present to be pushed.
hawking.7: The modern definition keeps his idea and sharpens it: a field is a physical quantity that has a value at every point in space. Some fields hold a single number at each point. A sports analyst’s heat map, recording how often a player stood on each patch of the MCG, is exactly that kind of field.
hawking.8: The fields in this Area of Study hold something richer at every point: an arrow. Its direction tells you the direction of the force on a suitable test object placed there; its length tells you the strength of that force. A field diagram, with its lines and arrowheads, is a map of those arrows — direction along the line, strength shown by how tightly the lines crowd together.
hawking.9: One drawing rule matters more than the rest: field lines never cross. If two lines met, the field there would point in two directions at once. No force can do that.
hawking.10: Connects A field diagram is the Unit 2 force diagram, extended. Instead of drawing the arrows on one object, the arrow is fixed in advance for every point in space. The precise definition, the test objects and the field-line rules open the core at What is a field?
hawking.11: Unit 3 introduces three of these maps. The gravitational field is sourced by mass and acts on mass. The electric field is sourced by charge and acts on charge. The magnetic field is sourced by moving charge — a current in a wire, or the orbiting and spinning electrons inside a bar magnet — and acts on moving charge. Figure 1.1 draws the three side by side. The family resemblance matters: one grammar, three dialects.
hawking.12: The differences carry the physics. Mass comes in one kind only — there is no negative mass — so gravity only ever attracts, and every gravitational field line points inward. In the language of Figure 1.1, a mass is purely a sink: lines flow in, never out.
hawking.13: Electric charge comes in two signs. Field lines flow out of positive charges (sources) and into negative charges (sinks), and either kind can stand alone — an isolated charge is a monopole. Two signs means electric forces can attract or repel.
hawking.14: Magnetism obeys the strangest rule of the three. No isolated north or south pole has ever been found. Cut a bar magnet in half and you get two complete magnets, each with both poles, all the way down to subatomic particles. Magnetic sources are dipoles only — a source and a sink locked together — which is why the magnet’s lines in Figure 1.1 leave the north face and loop round to the south.
hawking.15: This three-way contrast — gravity: monopoles of one sign; electricity: monopoles of both signs; magnetism: dipoles only — turns three things to memorise into one idea with three settings. Simulation 1.1 lets you test all three.
hawking.16: Connects This comparison becomes an examinable table — sources, directions, shapes, attraction and repulsion, monopoles and dipoles — in Comparing the three fields, and the exam harvests it dot point by dot point in Pass 3.
hawking.17: This is the first of four threads carried through the year. Lift a schoolbag from the floor to the desk and you do work against the gravitational field. That energy does not vanish. It is stored in the bag’s raised position, and the moment the bag slides off the desk it is handed back as kinetic energy, in full.
hawking.18: Every field in this Area of Study works the same way. Pull a positive and a negative charge apart and you store energy in their separation; let them go and that energy returns as motion. Position in a field is stored energy — potential energy — and the whole topic is double-entry bookkeeping: energy out of one account, into another, total unchanged.
hawking.19: The unit of account is the detail to note. Gravitational stores are tallied per kilogram of mass; electrical stores are tallied per coulomb of charge — and "energy per coulomb" is a quantity you have used since Unit 1 under its everyday name, voltage. One formula, W = qV not on sheet, says exactly this: the energy transformed when a charge q moves through a potential difference V — the formula sheet carries the same statement, folded into ½mv² = qV. Calculate nothing yet. This is Unit 1’s definition of voltage, read in reverse.
hawking.20: When a field is not uniform, you read the store off a graph, as the area under a force–distance curve — the same method Units 1&2 used for springs. One ledger, new kinds of deposit.
hawking.21: Connects Gravitational storage is banked in Gravity and orbits and electrical storage in Electric fields. Area of Study 1 runs the identical ledger for projectiles and collisions, and in Unit 4 the photoelectric effect uses voltage as a measuring instrument for single electrons.
hawking.22: Thread two is a shape. Newton imagined a cannon on a mountaintop above the atmosphere, fired horizontally. A slow cannonball arcs to the ground nearby; a faster one lands further off; and at about 7.9 km s⁻¹ something remarkable happens — the ball falls toward Earth exactly as fast as Earth’s surface curves away beneath it. It is falling forever, and missing forever. That is all an orbit is.
hawking.23: The Moon is Newton’s cannonball, fired long ago. Each second it falls about 1.4 mm toward Earth — the distance its curved path drops below the straight line it would otherwise take — and in that same second its sideways velocity carries it far enough that the drop never closes the gap. A satellite needs no engine to stay up. It needs sideways speed and a single inward force: gravity.
hawking.24: This corrects a common belief about astronauts. At the International Space Station’s altitude the gravitational field strength is still about 8.7 N kg⁻¹ — close to 90 per cent of its surface value — so the claim that there is no gravity in space is simply wrong. Astronauts float because the station and everyone in it are falling together. No surface pushes back on anyone, and that normal force is what you feel as weight. The state is called apparent weightlessness, and the word "apparent" carries the whole point.
hawking.25: The same mathematics turns up in another form. A charged particle moving across a magnetic field feels a force, named by F = qvB on formula sheet, that points at right angles to the particle’s velocity — always. A sideways force of constant size changes a path’s direction but never its speed, so the particle is bent into a circle, exactly as the satellite is. Whether gravity plays the inward role on a satellite or a magnetic field plays it on an electron in a synchrotron ring, it is one piece of circular-motion mathematics in two costumes — constant speed, never constant velocity, because the direction never stops turning.
hawking.26: Connects Orbits are built properly in Satellites and circling charges in Circular paths. Area of Study 1 supplies the circular-motion rule both obey — naming which force plays the inward, centripetal role reduces many of the year’s hardest problems to one.
hawking.27: Thread three: fields are not only for explaining nature. You can also use them to build. The starting point is the central fact of this Area of Study: a current-carrying wire placed across a magnetic field feels a force, named by F = nIlB on formula sheet. Reverse the current and you reverse the force.
hawking.28: Bend the wire into a rectangular coil on an axle between two magnet poles, and current runs up one side and down the other, so the two sides feel equal forces in opposite directions — a turning pair, and the coil rotates. The design has one flaw: half a turn later, the same pair of forces would turn it back again.
hawking.29: The remedy is the split-ring commutator — a copper ring cut in two that reverses the current through the coil every half-turn, so the turning effect always acts the same way. This is a DC motor, the machine inside every drill, fan and electric vehicle, and a diagram you will study in detail later. Run it in reverse — spin the coil with wind, water or steam — and a current is driven through the wire instead. That machine is a generator, the whole subject of Area of Study 3.
hawking.30: The other great field machine is the particle accelerator, and its operation is a two-line summary of this Area of Study: a uniform electric field adds kinetic energy to a charge along a straight line, and a uniform magnetic field bends the charge’s path without changing its speed. The Australian Synchrotron in Clayton chains the two together, holding electrons at more than 99.99 per cent of the speed of light.
hawking.31: Connects The motor — with the labelled split-ring commutator diagram examiners ask about almost every year — is built in Motors & accelerators. Area of Study 3 runs the identical hardware in reverse to supply the state’s electricity.
hawking.32: The last thread points beyond this Area of Study. Among its dot points is a small act of sorting: classify every field as static or changing, and as uniform or non-uniform. It looks like a minor matter. It is the most consequential classification of the year.
hawking.33: Changing magnetic fields drive currents in nearby conductors. That single fact, Faraday’s discovery of induction, is how almost every power station on Earth makes electricity, and it is Area of Study 3 in one sentence. Unit 4 then pushes the idea further: a changing electric field creates a changing magnetic field, which recreates the electric field, and the self-sustaining pair lets go of charges and wires altogether and travels through empty space. That travelling ripple of fields is light. The field, introduced as a bookkeeping trick for forces, turns out to be a thing in its own right.
hawking.34: A second loose thread is printed in the study design itself: the circular-path rule for charges holds only while the speed v is much less than c. Synchrotron electrons and GPS satellite clocks both fall outside that condition, and Unit 4’s relativity deals with it. A third: the practical investigation of motor torque — varying the current, the field and the number of loops — is a warm-up for the student-designed investigation that ends Unit 4.
hawking.35: Connects The accelerator endpoint of this topic is examined in Pass 3. Induction, electromagnetic waves and relativity belong to Area of Study 3 and Unit 4 — signposted now so that none of them arrives a stranger.
hawking.36: The whole Area of Study has now been surveyed once. Everything from here is the same content at higher resolution, and the book is built so the current resolution is always clear.

veritasium.0: Here's the question that runs underneath all of Unit 3 Area of Study 2 — and most of the rest of the year: how can one object move another without ever touching it? You might think that's a simple thing to answer. It isn't. This overview lays out the whole picture. You won't solve any of it yet; you're just here to see the shape of it.
veritasium.1: Drop your phone. Nothing visible reaches up to grab it — no string, no hand, no gust of air — and yet the instant you let go, something hauls it toward the floor at 9.8 m s⁻², every single time. Think about how strange that is.
veritasium.2: A compass needle, sealed inside its plastic case, swings to point north anywhere in Victoria. A balloon rubbed on your jumper drags hair toward it from a handsbreadth away. You might see three unrelated tricks here, but they share one puzzle: in every case, something moves with no contact at all.
veritasium.3: And this puzzle has bothered some of the greatest minds in science. Newton wrote down the law of gravitation that still steers spacecraft today — yet privately he thought the idea of bodies tugging on each other across empty space was absurd, and he flatly refused to say how it happened. This entire Area of Study — its official title is How do things move without contact? — is the modern answer to the thing Newton couldn’t bring himself to explain.
veritasium.4: And the answer turns out to be a single idea — the field — plus a year of working out what follows from it. This overview walks the topic end to end, so that when the detail lands in Pass 2, it lands in a landscape you already recognise.
veritasium.5: Connects Here's something you've actually been using since Year 11 without naming it: the 9.8 you multiply masses by carries the unit N kg⁻¹ — newtons of force for every kilogram you place at that point. That's a field strength. Pass 2 chapter 4 puts that unit at the very centre of Unit 3.
veritasium.6: The answer came from a direction nobody expected. Michael Faraday — a bookbinder’s apprentice who taught himself science between jobs — spent the 1830s watching iron filings line themselves up into arcs around magnets. You might look at that and see just a pattern. Faraday saw something far stranger: the magnet changes the space around it, and that altered space — the region he called a field — is real, there whether or not anything is sitting in it to be pushed.
veritasium.7: The modern definition keeps Faraday’s idea and sharpens it: a field is a physical quantity that has a value at every point in space. Some fields just hold a single number at each point — a sports analyst’s heat map, recording how often a player stood on each patch of the MCG, is exactly this kind of field.
veritasium.8: But the fields in this Area of Study hold something richer at every point: an arrow. The arrow’s direction tells you which way the force pushes on a suitable test object placed there, and its length tells you how strong that push is. A field diagram, with all its lines and arrowheads, is just a map of those arrows — direction along the line, strength shown by how tightly the lines bunch together.
veritasium.9: One drawing rule matters more than it looks: field lines never cross. Think about why. If two lines met, the field at that point would have to point in two directions at once — and no force can push two ways at the same time.
veritasium.10: Connects A field diagram is really the Unit 2 force diagram, scaled up: instead of drawing the arrows on one object, you work out the arrow in advance for every point in space. The precise definition, the test objects and the field-line rules open the core at What is a field?
veritasium.11: Unit 3 hands you three of these maps. The gravitational field is sourced by mass and acts on mass. The electric field is sourced by charge and acts on charge. The magnetic field is sourced by moving charge — a current in a wire, or the orbiting and spinning electrons inside a bar magnet — and acts on moving charge. Figure 1.1 draws the three side by side, and the family resemblance is the whole point: one grammar, three dialects.
veritasium.12: But the differences are where the physics lives. Mass comes in one kind only — there is no negative mass — so gravity only ever attracts, and every gravitational field line points inward. In the language of Figure 1.1, a mass is purely a sink: lines flow in, never out.
veritasium.13: Electric charge comes in two signs, and that changes everything. Field lines flow out of positive charges (sources) and into negative charges (sinks), and either kind can sit there entirely on its own — an isolated charge is called a monopole. Two signs means electric forces can attract or repel.
veritasium.14: Magnetism plays by the strangest rule of the three. No isolated north or south pole has ever been found — not once. Cut a bar magnet in half and you don’t get a north piece and a south piece; you get two complete magnets, each with both poles, and it stays that way all the way down to subatomic particles. Magnetic sources are dipoles only — a source and a sink permanently locked together — which is why the magnet’s lines in Figure 1.1 leave the north face and loop right around to the south.
veritasium.15: This three-way contrast — gravity: monopoles of one sign; electricity: monopoles of both signs; magnetism: dipoles only — turns the three maps from three separate things to memorise into one idea with three settings. Simulation 1.1 lets you test all three for yourself.
veritasium.16: Connects This comparison becomes an examinable table — sources, directions, shapes, attraction and repulsion, monopoles and dipoles — in Comparing the three fields, and the exam harvests it dot point by dot point in Pass 3.
veritasium.17: This is the first of four threads we carry right through the year. When you lift a schoolbag from the floor to the desk, you do work against the gravitational field. That energy doesn’t just vanish: it’s stored in the bag’s raised position, and the moment the bag slides off the desk, you get every bit of it back as kinetic energy.
veritasium.18: And here's the thing — every field in this Area of Study works exactly the same way. Pull a positive and a negative charge apart and you store energy in their separation; let them go and that energy comes straight back as motion. Position in a field is stored energy — potential energy — and the whole topic is really double-entry bookkeeping: energy out of one account, into another, total unchanged.
veritasium.19: The detail to watch is the accounting unit. Gravitational stores are tallied per kilogram of mass; electrical stores are tallied per coulomb of charge — and "energy per coulomb" is something you’ve used since Unit 1 under its everyday name, voltage. One formula, W = qV not on sheet, says exactly this: the energy transformed when a charge q moves through a potential difference V — and the formula sheet carries the same statement folded into ½mv² = qV. Don’t calculate anything yet; this is just Unit 1’s definition of voltage read backwards.
veritasium.20: When a field isn’t uniform, you read the store off a graph — the area under a force–distance curve, the very same method Units 1&2 used for springs. One ledger, new kinds of deposit.
veritasium.21: Connects Gravitational storage is banked in Gravity and orbits and electrical storage in Electric fields. Area of Study 1 runs the identical ledger for projectiles and collisions, and in Unit 4 the photoelectric effect will use voltage as a measuring instrument for single electrons.
veritasium.22: Thread two is a shape. Newton pictured a cannon on a mountaintop above the atmosphere, fired sideways. A slow cannonball arcs to the ground nearby; a faster one lands further out; and at about 7.9 km s⁻¹ something remarkable happens — the ball falls toward Earth at exactly the rate Earth’s surface curves away beneath it. It’s falling continuously, and missing continuously. That, and nothing more, is what an orbit is.
veritasium.23: The Moon is Newton’s cannonball, fired a long time ago. Every second it falls about 1.4 mm toward Earth — that’s how far its curved path drops below the straight line it would otherwise travel — and in that same second its sideways velocity carries it far enough that the drop never closes the gap. A satellite needs no engine to stay up. It needs sideways speed and one inward force: gravity.
veritasium.24: And this fixes a belief almost everyone gets wrong. At the International Space Station’s altitude the gravitational field strength is still about 8.7 N kg⁻¹ — nearly 90 per cent of its surface value — so the idea that there’s no gravity in space is simply false. Astronauts float because the station and everyone inside it are falling together: no surface pushes up on anyone, and that normal force is what you actually feel as weight. The proper name for this is apparent weightlessness, and the word "apparent" is doing real work.
veritasium.25: Now watch the same mathematics show up in disguise. A charged particle moving across a magnetic field feels a force, given by F = qvB on formula sheet, that points at right angles to the particle’s velocity — always. A sideways force of constant size changes a path’s direction but never its speed, so the particle gets steered into a circle, exactly like the satellite. Whether the inward role is played by gravity on a satellite or by a magnetic field on an electron in a synchrotron ring, it’s one piece of circular-motion mathematics wearing two costumes — constant speed, never constant velocity, because the direction never stops changing.
veritasium.26: Connects Orbits are built properly in Satellites and circling charges in Circular paths. Area of Study 1 supplies the circular-motion rule both obey — identifying which force plays the inward, centripetal role reduces many of the year’s hardest problems to one.
veritasium.27: Thread three: fields aren’t only for explaining nature — you can use them to build things. The starting point is the central fact of this Area of Study: a current-carrying wire placed across a magnetic field feels a force, given by F = nIlB on formula sheet. Reverse the current and you reverse the force.
veritasium.28: Bend the wire into a rectangular coil on an axle between two magnet poles, and current runs up one side of the coil and down the other — so the two sides feel equal forces in opposite directions, a turning pair, and the coil spins. But there’s a flaw built right in: half a turn later, that same pair of forces would just turn it back the other way.
veritasium.29: The fix is the split-ring commutator — a copper ring cut in two that flips the current through the coil every half-turn, so the turning effect always pushes the same way. That’s a DC motor, the machine inside every drill, fan and electric vehicle, and you’ll study its diagram in detail later. Run it backwards — spin the coil with wind, water or steam — and instead of drawing current it drives one through the wire: that machine is a generator, the whole subject of Area of Study 3.
veritasium.30: The other great field machine is the particle accelerator, and its whole operation is a two-line summary of this Area of Study: a uniform electric field adds kinetic energy to a charge along a straight line, and a uniform magnetic field bends the charge’s path without touching its speed. The Australian Synchrotron in Clayton chains the two together, holding electrons at more than 99.99 per cent of the speed of light.
veritasium.31: Connects The motor — with the labelled split-ring commutator diagram examiners ask about almost every year — is built in Motors & accelerators. Area of Study 3 runs the identical hardware in reverse to supply the state’s electricity.
veritasium.32: The last thread points beyond this Area of Study. Among its dot points is a small sorting task: classify every field as static or changing, and as uniform or non-uniform. It looks trivial. It is, in fact, the most consequential classification of the whole year.
veritasium.33: Changing magnetic fields drive currents in nearby conductors — and that one fact, Faraday’s discovery of induction, is how almost every power station on Earth makes electricity. That’s Area of Study 3 in a single sentence. Unit 4 then pushes the idea further than you’d expect: a changing electric field creates a changing magnetic field, which recreates the electric field, and the self-sustaining pair tears free of charges and wires altogether and travels through empty space. That travelling ripple of fields is light. The field, which we introduced as a bookkeeping trick for forces, turns out to be a real thing in its own right.
veritasium.34: A second loose thread is printed right into the study design: the circular-path rule for charges only holds while the speed v is much less than c. Synchrotron electrons and GPS satellite clocks both break that condition, and Unit 4’s relativity is what deals with it. A third: the practical investigation of motor torque — varying the current, the field and the number of loops — is a warm-up for the student-designed investigation that closes Unit 4.
veritasium.35: Connects The accelerator endpoint of this topic is examined in Pass 3. Induction, electromagnetic waves and relativity belong to Area of Study 3 and Unit 4 — signposted now so that none of them arrives unfamiliar.
veritasium.36: So that’s the whole Area of Study, surveyed once. Everything from here is the same content at higher resolution, and the book is built so you always know which resolution you’re looking at.

Michael Faraday — a bookbinder’s apprentice who taught himself the secrets of nature between his labours — passed the 1830s watching iron filings arrange themselves into arcs about the magnets. Where others saw only a pattern, Faraday proclaimed something greater: the magnet alters the space about it, and that altered condition of space — the region he named a field — is real, present whether or not aught stands there to be acted upon.
greekepic.7: The modern decree keeps his idea and sharpens it: a field is a physical quantity holding a value at every point in space. Some fields cradle a single number at each point — a sports analyst’s heat map, recording how often a player held each patch of the MCG, is exactly such a field.
greekepic.8: But the fields of this Area of Study cradle something richer at every point: an arrow. The arrow’s direction declares the direction of the force borne upon a suitable test object set there, and its length declares the strength of that force. A field diagram, with all its lines and arrowheads, is a map of those arrows — direction along the line, strength shown by how closely the lines are drawn together.
greekepic.9: One rule of the drawing carries true weight: field lines never cross. For did two lines meet, the field at that point would point two ways at once, and no force can act in two directions at once.
greekepic.10: Connects A field diagram is the Unit 2 force diagram made greater: rather than drawing the arrows acting upon one object alone, the arrow is appointed beforehand for every point in space. The precise definition, the test objects and the field-line rules throw open the core at What is a field?
greekepic.11: Unit 3 brings forth three of these maps. The gravitational field is sourced by mass and acts upon mass. The electric field is sourced by charge and acts upon charge. The magnetic field is sourced by moving charge — a current in a wire, or the orbiting and spinning electrons within a bar magnet — and acts upon moving charge. Figure 1.1 draws the three side by side, and the family resemblance is no small thing: one grammar, three dialects.
greekepic.12: The differences bear the physics upon their shoulders. Mass comes in one kind only — there is no negative mass — so gravity forever attracts and never repels, and every gravitational field line points inward. In the language of Figure 1.1, a mass is purely a sink: the lines flow in, and never out.
greekepic.13: Electric charge comes in two signs. Field lines flow out of positive charges (sources) and into negative charges (sinks), and either kind may stand alone — a charge so isolated is called a monopole. Two signs means electric forces may attract or repel.
greekepic.14: Magnetism follows the strangest law of the three. No lone north or south pole has ever been found: a bar magnet cleft in half yields two whole magnets, each with both poles, down even to the subatomic particles. Magnetic sources are dipoles and dipoles only — a source and a sink wedded forever — which is why the magnet’s lines in Figure 1.1 leave the north face and loop around to the south.
greekepic.15: This threefold contrast — gravity: monopoles of one sign; electricity: monopoles of both signs; magnetism: dipoles only — turns the three maps from three separate things to be committed to memory into one idea wearing three faces. Simulation 1.1 lets all three be put to the test.
greekepic.16: Connects This comparison becomes an examinable table — sources, directions, shapes, attraction and repulsion, monopoles and dipoles — in Comparing the three fields, and the exam harvests it dot point by dot point in Pass 3.
greekepic.17: Here is the first of four threads drawn through the whole year. To lift a schoolbag from the floor to the desk is to do work against the gravitational field. That energy does not perish: it is stored in the bag’s raised station, and the very moment the bag slides off the desk it is given back as kinetic energy, in full.
greekepic.18: Every field of this Area of Study labours in the same fashion. To draw a positive and a negative charge apart is to store energy in their parting; to release them is to give that energy back to motion. Position in a field is stored energy — potential energy — and the whole topic is a keeping of ledgers: energy out of one account, into another, the total never changed.
greekepic.19: The unit of the reckoning is the detail to mark well. Gravitational stores are tallied per kilogram of mass; electrical stores are tallied per coulomb of charge — and “energy per coulomb” is a quantity you have wielded since Unit 1 under its common name, voltage. One formula, W = qV not on sheet, declares exactly this: the energy transformed when a charge q moves through a potential difference V — and the formula sheet bears the same decree folded into ½mv² = qV. Nothing is yet to be reckoned; this is Unit 1’s definition of voltage read in reverse.
greekepic.20: When a field is not uniform, the store is read upon the page, as the area beneath a force–distance graph — the very method Units 1&2 used for springs. One ledger, new kinds of deposit.
greekepic.21: Connects Gravitational storage is banked in Gravity and orbits and electrical storage in Electric fields. Area of Study 1 runs the selfsame ledger for projectiles and collisions, and in Unit 4 the photoelectric effect shall wield voltage as a measuring instrument for single electrons.
greekepic.22: Thread the second is a shape. Newton imagined a cannon upon a mountaintop above the breath of the atmosphere, fired straight along the horizon. A slow cannonball arcs to the ground close by; a swifter one falls further off; and at some 7.9 km s⁻¹ a marvel comes to pass — the ball falls toward the Earth exactly as fast as the Earth’s surface curves away beneath it. It is forever falling, and forever missing: and that is all an orbit is.
greekepic.23: The Moon is Newton’s cannonball, fired in ages long past. In every second it falls about 1.4 mm toward the Earth — the distance its curved path drops below the straight line it would otherwise hold — and in that same second its sideways velocity bears it far enough that the falling never brings it nearer. A satellite asks no engine to stay aloft; it asks sideways speed and one inward force alone: gravity.
greekepic.24: This sets right a belief widely held about the astronauts. At the altitude of the International Space Station the gravitational field strength is still about 8.7 N kg⁻¹ — near 90 per cent of its value at the surface — so the saying that there is no gravity in space speaks falsely. The astronauts float because the station and all within it are falling together: no surface presses a normal force upon anyone, and it is the normal force that is felt as weight. This state is named apparent weightlessness, and the word “apparent” bears great weight.
greekepic.25: The same mathematics rises again in another guise. A charged particle moving across a magnetic field bears a force, named by F = qvB on formula sheet, that points at right angles to the particle’s velocity — always. A sideways force of unchanging size alters a path’s direction but never its speed, so the particle is steered into a circle, even as the satellite is. Whether the inward part be played by gravity upon a satellite or by a magnetic field upon an electron in a synchrotron ring, it is one piece of circular-motion mathematics in two forms — constant speed, never constant velocity, for the direction never ceases its turning.
greekepic.26: Connects Orbits are built in their full measure in Satellites and circling charges in Circular paths. Area of Study 1 furnishes the circular-motion rule both obey — and to name which force plays the inward, centripetal part is to reduce many of the year’s hardest labours to one.
greekepic.27: Thread the third: fields are not only for the telling of nature; they may also be set to building. The starting point is the central truth of this Area of Study: a current-carrying wire set across a magnetic field bears a force, named by F = nIlB on formula sheet. Reverse the current, and you reverse the force.
greekepic.28: Bend the wire into a rectangular coil upon an axle between two poles of a magnet, and the current travels up one side of the coil and down the other, so the two sides bear equal forces in opposite directions — a turning pair, and the coil rotates. Yet one flaw lurks within this design: half a turn later, the same pair of forces would turn it back the other way.
greekepic.29: The remedy is the split-ring commutator — a copper ring cleft in two that reverses the current through the coil every half-turn, so the turning effect forever acts in the one direction. This is a DC motor, the engine within every drill, fan and electric vehicle, a diagram to be studied in detail hereafter. Run it in reverse — the coil spun by wind, by water or by steam — and a current is driven through the wire instead: that machine is a generator, the whole matter of Area of Study 3.
greekepic.30: The other great field machine is the particle accelerator, whose working is a two-line summary of this Area of Study: a uniform electric field adds kinetic energy to a charge along a straight line, and a uniform magnetic field bends the charge’s path without altering its speed. The Australian Synchrotron in Clayton chains the two together, holding electrons at more than 99.99 per cent of the speed of light.
greekepic.31: Connects The motor — bearing the labelled split-ring commutator diagram the examiners ask for in well-nigh every year — is built in Motors & accelerators. Area of Study 3 runs the selfsame hardware in reverse to furnish the state with its electricity.
greekepic.32: The last thread points beyond this Area of Study. Among its dot points lies a small act of sorting: classify every field as static or changing, and as uniform or non-uniform. This seems a minor matter; it is the most consequential classification of the year.
greekepic.33: Changing magnetic fields drive currents in conductors nearby — that single fact, Faraday’s discovery of induction, is how well-nigh every power station upon the Earth begets electricity, and it is Area of Study 3 in a single sentence. Then Unit 4 carries the idea further still: a changing electric field creates a changing magnetic field, which recreates the electric field, and the self-sustaining pair breaks free of charges and wires altogether and journeys through the empty void. That travelling ripple of fields is light. The field, brought forth as a keeping of ledgers for forces, proves to be a thing in its own right.
greekepic.34: A second loose thread is printed in the study design itself: the circular-path rule for charges holds sure only while the speed v is much less than c. Synchrotron electrons and GPS satellite clocks alike lie outside that condition, and Unit 4’s relativity takes it up. A third: the practical investigation of motor torque — varying the current, the field and the number of loops — is a forerunner to the student-designed investigation that brings Unit 4 to its close.
greekepic.35: Connects The accelerator endpoint of this topic is examined in Pass 3. Induction, electromagnetic waves and relativity belong to Area of Study 3 and Unit 4 — signposted now so that none of them shall arrive a stranger.
greekepic.36: The whole Area of Study has now been surveyed once over. Everything from here onward is the same content at higher resolution, and the book is so wrought that the present resolution is always plain to see.
greekepic.37: Pass 2 is the core, in five chapters meant to be read the night before each topic is taught: the field idea; gravity and orbits; electric fields; magnetic fields; motors and accelerators. It holds every examinable equation with its formula-sheet status, the standard situations, and quick checks to confirm each idea has truly been grasped.
greekepic.38: Pass 3 is for the season of exams. It walks the sixteen key-knowledge dot points in study-design order — the very order the exam is written from — with the subtleties, the classic traps, the assessors’ counsel and exam-style questions with full solutions. Between the passes, depth links like the row below move up and down, and this page stays one click away whenever the detail begins to obscure the overall shape.
greekepic.39: Connects Begin Pass 2 · The field idea when you are ready, and bookmark Pass 3 · Fields and interactions for the exam period.

cnpop.0: 整个第三单元第二学习领域——还有这一年剩下的一大半内容——其实都从一个问题里长出来：一个物体怎么能在不碰它的情况下推动另一个物体？这篇概览先把全景图甩给你看。现在啥都不用算，先扫一眼地形就行。
cnpop.1: 手一松，手机往下掉。可你瞅瞅，没有任何看得见的东西伸上来接它——没绳子、没手、也没气流——可就在松手那一瞬间，有个东西把它往地板上拽，加速度死死咬在 9.8 m s⁻²，每次都一模一样。
cnpop.2: 一根指南针，明明被密封在塑料壳里，在维多利亚州哪个角落它都乖乖指向北方。一个气球在毛衣上蹭两下，隔着一掌远就能把头发吸过来。这三件小破事其实共享同一个谜题：每一次，运动都是在零接触的情况下凭空冒出来的。
cnpop.3: 这道谜题可把大佬们折腾惨了。牛顿写下了那条直到今天还在给航天器导航的万有引力定律，可他私底下觉得“物体能隔着真空互相作用”这种说法简直离谱，干脆拒绝解释它到底咋发生的。整个学习领域——它的官方标题就是How do things move without contact?——就是现代物理给出的回答。
cnpop.4: 这个回答其实就一个核心点子：场，外加随之而来的一整年的连锁后果。这篇概览把整个主题从头到尾给你铺一遍，这样等第二遍（Pass 2）的细节砸过来时，你早就对这片地形熟门熟路了。
cnpop.5: Connects 其实从十一年级开始你就一直在用一种场强了：9.8 这个值乘上质量，带的单位是 N kg⁻¹——也就是放在那个点上每千克所受的牛顿数。Pass 2 chapter 4 就把这个单位顶到了整个第三单元的正中央。
cnpop.6: 答案是从一个谁也想不到的方向冒出来的。迈克尔·法拉第——一个靠打工间隙自学科学的装订工学徒——在 1830 年代盯着铁屑围着磁铁排成一道道弧线看了一整个十年。别人只看到一个图案，法拉第却脑洞大开：磁铁改变了它周围的空间，而这种被改变的空间状态——他管这块区域叫field（场）——是真实存在的，不管那儿有没有东西被它作用，它都在。
cnpop.7: 现代的定义保留了他的点子，又把它打磨得更精确：场就是一个在空间每一点上都有取值的物理量。有些场每个点上只挂一个数字——比如体育分析师画的热力图，记录一个球员在 MCG 球场每块草皮上待了多久，这就是典型的这一类场。
cnpop.8: 而这个学习领域里的场，每个点上挂的东西更带劲：一支箭头。箭头的方向告诉你，放在那儿的合适测试物体会被往哪个方向推；箭头的长度则告诉你这个力有多猛。一张场图，连同它的线和箭头，就是这些箭头的一张地图——方向沿着线走，强弱靠线挤得多密来表示。
cnpop.9: 有一条画图规则可不是闹着玩的：场线永远不能相交。要是两条线撞一块儿了，那一点上的场就同时有两个方向了——可没有哪个力能一口气朝两个方向使劲。
cnpop.10: Connects 一张场图，说白了就是第二单元那张受力图的升级版：以前你只画作用在某一个物体上的那些箭头，现在是给空间里每一个点都提前把箭头定死。精确定义、测试物体、场线规则这些硬核内容，从 What is a field? 这儿正式开门。
cnpop.11: 第三单元一口气端出三张这样的地图。引力场由质量产生、也作用在质量上。电场由电荷产生、也作用在电荷上。磁场则由运动的电荷产生——比如导线里的电流，又比如条形磁铁内部那些绕转加自旋的电子——它也作用在运动的电荷上。Figure 1.1 把这三张图并排画给你看，那股子家族脸的相似劲儿可大有讲究：一套语法，三种方言。
cnpop.12: 真正藏着物理的，是它们之间的差别。质量只有一种——这世上没有负质量——所以引力永远只会吸引，每一条引力场线都老老实实地指向里头。用 Figure 1.1 的话说，一个质量纯纯就是个汇（sink）：线只进不出。
cnpop.13: 电荷可就有两种符号了。场线从正电荷（源）里往外喷，再扎进负电荷（汇）里，而且哪一种都能单独存在——一个孤零零的电荷叫单极子（monopole）。两种符号意味着电力既能吸引又能排斥。
cnpop.14: 磁，遵守的是三者里最离谱的一条规则。从没有人逮到过一个孤立的北极或南极：把一根条形磁铁一刀切两半，你会得到两块完整的磁铁，每块都南北极俱全，一路切到亚原子粒子都是这样。磁的源头只能是偶极子（dipole）——一个源和一个汇永远捆死在一起——这也正是为啥 Figure 1.1 里磁铁的线会从北极面跑出来、再绕一圈拐回南极。
cnpop.15: 这个三方对比——引力：只有一种符号的单极子；电：两种符号的单极子；磁：只有偶极子——一下子把这三张图从“三样要死记硬背的东西”变成了“一个点子的三档设置”。Simulation 1.1 让你把这三档全都亲手试一遍。
cnpop.16: Connects 这个对比到后面会变成一张可以拿来考你的表格——源、方向、形状、吸引和排斥、单极子和偶极子——出现在 Comparing the three fields 里，而考试会一个考点一个考点地把它收割干净，详见 Pass 3。
cnpop.17: 这是贯穿一整年的四条线里的第一条。把书包从地板拎到桌上，你是在对抗引力场做功。这份能量可没凭空蒸发：它被存进了书包被抬高的那个位置里，等书包从桌上一滑下来，这能量就原封不动、一分不少地以动能的形式还回来了。
cnpop.18: 这个学习领域里的每一个场，玩的都是这一套。把一个正电荷和一个负电荷分开，能量就存进它们之间的间隔里；把它们一松，能量又乖乖变回运动。在场里的位置就是被存起来的能量——势能——而整个主题说穿了就是复式记账：能量从一个账户出去、进到另一个账户，总额一分不差。
cnpop.19: 要留心的细节是记账的单位。引力储能是按每千克质量来记的；电储能则是按每库仑电荷来记——而“每库仑的能量”这个量，你从第一单元就一直在用，只不过它当时叫的是大白话名字：电压。一条公式 W = qV not on sheet 把这事儿说得明明白白：当一个电荷 q 穿过电势差 V 时被转换的能量——公式表上把同一句话折叠进了 ½mv² = qV。现在啥都不用算；这不过是把第一单元里电压的定义倒着读一遍而已。
cnpop.20: 当场不均匀的时候，储能就靠看图来读，也就是力—距离图线下方的面积——跟第一、二单元处理弹簧用的是同一招。同一本账本，新的存款种类罢了。
cnpop.21: Connects 引力储能存进 Gravity and orbits，电储能存进 Electric fields。第一学习领域给抛体和碰撞跑的是同一本账本，而到了第四单元，光电效应会把电压当成给单个电子测量的尺子。
cnpop.22: 第二条线是个形状。牛顿脑补了一门架在大气层之上山顶的大炮，水平开火。慢吞吞的炮弹划个弧、砸在附近；快一点的落得更远；而当速度飙到约 7.9 km s⁻¹ 时，神奇的事情发生了——炮弹朝地球下落的速度，正好等于地表在它脚下弯下去的速度。它在不停地下落，又在不停地“没砸到”地面：轨道说到底就这么回事。
cnpop.23: 月亮就是牛顿那门大炮的炮弹，只不过是很久很久以前开的火。每一秒它都朝地球落下约 1.4 mm——这就是它的弯曲轨迹比那条本该走的直线低下去的距离——而就在同一秒，它横向的速度又把它带得足够远，让这一落永远拉不近距离。一颗卫星想待在天上根本不需要发动机；它要的只是横向速度，外加一个唯一的向内的力：引力。
cnpop.24: 这正好纠正一个关于宇航员的常见误会。在国际空间站那个高度，引力场强其实还有约 8.7 N kg⁻¹——差不多是地表值的 90%——所以“太空里没有引力”这句话是错的。宇航员之所以飘着，是因为空间站和里头的每一个人都在一起下落：没有任何表面对谁施加法向力，而法向力正是被你当成体重感受到的那个东西。这种状态叫做表观失重（apparent weightlessness），而“表观”这俩字大有深意。
cnpop.25: 同一套数学，换了个马甲又来了。一个带电粒子横穿磁场运动时会受到一个力，由 F = qvB on formula sheet 命名，这个力永远——注意是永远——垂直于粒子的速度。一个大小恒定的侧向力只改路径的方向、从不改它的速率，于是粒子被掰进了一个圆里，跟卫星的玩法一模一样。不管这个向内的角色是由作用在卫星上的引力来演，还是由作用在同步加速器环里电子上的磁场来演，它都是同一块圆周运动数学的两种形态——速率恒定，速度却永远不恒定，因为方向一刻都没停止过改变。
cnpop.26: Connects 轨道在 Satellites 里被正经搭起来，绕圈的电荷在 Circular paths 里。第一学习领域提供了它俩都得遵守的圆周运动规则——搞清楚是哪个力在演那个向内的、向心的角色，能把这一年里好多最硬的题一下子打回原形。
cnpop.27: 第三条线：场可不只是用来解释大自然的；它还能拿来造东西。出发点是这个学习领域里那条核心事实：一根载流导线横放在磁场里会受到一个力，由 F = nIlB on formula sheet 命名。把电流方向一反，力的方向也跟着反过来。
cnpop.28: 当你把导线弯成一个套在轴上、夹在两个磁极之间的矩形线圈，电流就从线圈的一侧往上走、从另一侧往下走，于是这两侧受到大小相等、方向相反的力——一对转动力偶，线圈就转起来了。可这设计有个硬伤：再转过半圈，同一对力又会把它往回掰。
cnpop.29: 解药就是换向器（split-ring commutator）——一个被切成两半的铜环，每转半圈就把流过线圈的电流反一次方向，这样转动的效果就始终朝同一个方向使劲。这就是直流电动机，藏在每一把电钻、每一台风扇、每一辆电动车里的那台机器，后面会有一张图带你细抠。要是把它倒着开——线圈被风、水或蒸汽带着转——那就反过来在导线里驱动出一股电流：那台机器叫发电机，正是第三学习领域的全部主题。
cnpop.30: 另一台重量级的场机器是粒子加速器，它的工作原理就是这个学习领域的两行总结：一个均匀电场沿直线给电荷添动能，一个均匀磁场则在不改变电荷速率的前提下把它的路径掰弯。位于克莱顿（Clayton）的澳大利亚同步加速器把这两招串到一起，把电子摁在光速的 99.99% 以上死死拽住。
cnpop.31: Connects 这台电动机——连同那张几乎年年被考官拿来出题的、标好换向器的图——在 Motors & accelerators 里被搭建出来。第三学习领域则把这套一模一样的硬件倒着开，来给整个州供电。
cnpop.32: 最后一条线指向了这个学习领域之外。它的考点里有一个看似不起眼的分类小动作：把每一个场归类成静态的还是变化的、均匀的还是非均匀的。看着像鸡毛蒜皮，可它其实是这一年里最有分量的一次分类。
cnpop.33: 变化的磁场会在附近的导体里驱动出电流——就这一个事实，也就是法拉第发现的电磁感应，是地球上几乎每一座电站发电的原理，一句话就把第三学习领域说完了。第四单元接着把这点子又往前推了一大步：一个变化的电场造出一个变化的磁场，磁场又重新造出电场，这对自给自足的搭档干脆从电荷和导线身上彻底挣脱出来，穿过真空一路狂奔。这股奔跑着的场的涟漪，就是光。场，一开始只是被当成给力记账用的工具，到头来竟然证明它自己就是个实打实的东西。
cnpop.34: 第二条没收尾的线，明晃晃地印在课程设计文件里：电荷的圆周路径规则，只有在速率 v 远小于 c 时才保证成立。同步加速器里的电子、还有 GPS 卫星上的钟，俩都落在这个条件之外，而第四单元的相对论正是来收拾这个的。第三条：电动机转矩的实践探究——改变电流、磁场、还有线圈匝数——是第四单元结尾那个学生自主设计探究的前菜。
cnpop.35: Connects 这个主题里加速器这个终点站，会在 Pass 3 里被考。电磁感应、电磁波和相对论归第三学习领域和第四单元管——现在先给你竖块路牌，免得到时候它们冷不丁冒出来把你吓一跳。
cnpop.36: 整个学习领域到这儿就被扫过一遍了。从这往后全是同样的内容、只是分辨率拉高了，而这本书的结构会让你随时清楚自己正待在哪一档分辨率上。
cnpop.37: Pass 2 是核心，分成五章，专门设计成在每个主题开讲的前一晚拿来读：场的点子；引力与轨道；电场；磁场；电动机与加速器。它装下了每一条要考的方程、以及它在不在公式表上、各种标准情境，还有用来确认每个点子你真的弄懂了的小测。
cnpop.38: Pass 3 是给考试季备的。它按课程设计的顺序——也就是出题人写卷子的顺序——把十六个核心知识考点一个个走一遍，带上那些微妙之处、经典陷阱、考官建议，还有配满整解答的考试风格题。在各遍之间，像下面那一行这样的深度链接可以上上下下地切换，而这一页永远只差一次点击就能回来，每当细节开始挡住你看整体形状的视线时就用它。
cnpop.39: Connects 准备好了就从 Pass 2 · The field idea 开干，再把 Pass 3 · Fields and interactions 收藏好，留给考试期间用。

confucius-en.0: The Master said. The whole of Unit 3 Area of Study 2 — and much of the year that follows — grows from one question: how one object may move another without touching it. Here the whole picture is laid before you. Solve nothing yet; for now it is enough to survey the land.
confucius-en.1: Release a phone, and it falls. Nothing you can see reaches up to take it — no string, no hand, no current of air — yet the instant it is freed, something draws it toward the floor at 9.8 m s⁻¹, and so it is in every case.
confucius-en.2: A compass needle, sealed within its plastic case, turns to the north anywhere in Victoria. A balloon rubbed upon a jumper draws hair to itself from a handsbreadth away. These three small observations share one riddle: in each, motion arises with no contact at all.
confucius-en.3: This riddle has troubled the greatest of minds. Newton set down the law of gravitation that still guides spacecraft, yet in private he held the notion of bodies acting on one another across empty space to be an absurdity, and would not say how it came to pass. The whole of this Area of Study — its proper title is How do things move without contact? — is our age’s answer.
confucius-en.4: That answer is a single idea, the field, and a year of its consequences. This overview presents the topic from end to end, so that when the detail arrives in Pass 2, it arrives upon ground already familiar to you.
confucius-en.5: Connects A field strength has served you since Year 11: the value 9.8, multiplied by masses, carries the unit N kg⁻¹ — newtons of force for every kilogram set at that point. Pass 2 chapter 4 makes that very unit the centre of Unit 3.
confucius-en.6: The answer came from an unlikely quarter. Michael Faraday — a bookbinder’s apprentice who taught himself science between his labours — spent the 1830s watching iron filings arrange themselves into arcs about magnets. Where others saw only a pattern, Faraday saw further: the magnet alters the space around it, and that altered condition of space — the region he named a field — is real, present whether or not anything stands there to be acted upon.
confucius-en.7: Our age keeps his idea and refines it: a field is a physical quantity that holds a value at every point in space. Some fields keep but a single number at each point — a sports analyst’s heat map, recording how often a player occupied each patch of the MCG, is exactly such a field.
confucius-en.8: The fields of this Area of Study keep something richer at every point: an arrow. The arrow’s direction tells the direction of the force borne by a fitting test object set there, and its length tells the strength of that force. A field diagram, with its lines and arrowheads, is a map of those arrows — direction along the line, strength shown by how closely the lines are drawn together.
confucius-en.9: One rule of drawing carries true weight: field lines never cross. Were two lines to meet, the field at that point would point two ways at once — and no force can act in two directions at once.
confucius-en.10: Connects A field diagram is the Unit 2 force diagram carried further: rather than drawing the arrows that act upon one object, the arrow is settled beforehand for every point in space. The precise definition, the test objects and the field-line rules open the core at What is a field?
confucius-en.11: Unit 3 sets before you three such maps. The gravitational field is sourced by mass and acts on mass. The electric field is sourced by charge and acts on charge. The magnetic field is sourced by moving charge — a current in a wire, or the orbiting and spinning electrons within a bar magnet — and acts on moving charge. Figure 1.1 draws the three side by side, and the family likeness is no small thing: one grammar, three dialects.
confucius-en.12: The differences carry the physics. Mass occurs in one kind only — there is no negative mass — so gravity only ever attracts, and every gravitational field line points inward. In the terms of Figure 1.1, a mass is purely a sink: lines flow in, never out.
confucius-en.13: Electric charge occurs in two signs. Field lines flow out of positive charges (sources) and into negative charges (sinks), and either kind may stand alone — an isolated charge is called a monopole. Two signs mean electric forces may attract or repel.
confucius-en.14: Magnetism follows the strangest rule of the three. No isolated north or south pole has ever been found: a bar magnet cut in half yields two whole magnets, each with both poles, and so it goes down to the subatomic. Magnetic sources are dipoles only — a source and a sink forever paired — which is why the magnet’s lines in Figure 1.1 leave the north face and loop around to the south.
confucius-en.15: This threefold contrast — gravity: monopoles of one sign; electricity: monopoles of both signs; magnetism: dipoles only — turns the three maps from three separate things to be memorised into one idea worn three ways. Simulation 1.1 lets all three be put to the test.
confucius-en.16: Connects This comparison becomes an examinable table — sources, directions, shapes, attraction and repulsion, monopoles and dipoles — in Comparing the three fields, and the exam gathers it dot point by dot point in Pass 3.
confucius-en.17: Here is the first of four threads carried through the year. To lift a schoolbag from the floor to the desk is to do work against the gravitational field. That energy does not vanish: it is stored in the bag’s raised position, and the moment the bag slides off the desk, it is returned as kinetic energy, in full.
confucius-en.18: Every field in this Area of Study keeps the same account. To separate a positive and a negative charge is to store energy in their separation; to release them is to return that energy to motion. Position in a field is stored energy — potential energy — and the whole topic is double-entry bookkeeping: energy out of one account, into another, the total unchanged.
confucius-en.19: The unit of account is the detail worth noting. Gravitational stores are tallied per kilogram of mass; electrical stores are tallied per coulomb of charge — and “energy per coulomb” is a quantity you have used since Unit 1 under its common name, voltage. One formula, W = qV not on sheet, says just this: the energy transformed when a charge q moves through a potential difference V — and the formula sheet carries the same saying folded into ½mv² = qV. Calculate nothing yet; this is Unit 1’s definition of voltage read in reverse.
confucius-en.20: Where a field is not uniform, the store is read by the eye, as the area under a force–distance graph — the very method Units 1&2 used for springs. One ledger, new kinds of deposit.
confucius-en.21: Connects Gravitational storage is banked in Gravity and orbits and electrical storage in Electric fields. Area of Study 1 keeps the selfsame ledger for projectiles and collisions, and in Unit 4 the photoelectric effect will use voltage as a measuring instrument for single electrons.
confucius-en.22: The second thread is a shape. Newton imagined a cannon upon a mountaintop above the atmosphere, fired level. A slow ball arcs to the ground nearby; a swifter one lands further off; and at about 7.9 km s⁻¹ a notable thing comes to pass — the ball falls toward Earth exactly as fast as Earth’s surface curves away beneath it. It is falling without end, and missing without end: that, and nothing more, is an orbit.
confucius-en.23: The Moon is Newton’s cannonball, fired long ago. In every second it falls about 1.4 mm toward Earth — the distance its curved path drops below the straight line it would otherwise keep — and in that same second its sideways velocity carries it far enough that the drop never brings it nearer. A satellite needs no engine to stay aloft; it needs sideways speed and a single inward force: gravity.
confucius-en.24: This corrects a belief widely held about astronauts. At the altitude of the International Space Station the gravitational field strength is still about 8.7 N kg⁻¹ — near 90 per cent of its value at the surface — so to say there is no gravity in space is to say what is untrue. Astronauts float because the station and all within it are falling together: no surface presses upon anyone with a normal force, and it is the normal force that is felt as weight. This state is named apparent weightlessness, and the word “apparent” is no idle word.
confucius-en.25: The same mathematics appears in another guise. A charged particle moving across a magnetic field bears a force, named by F = qvB on formula sheet, that points at right angles to the particle’s velocity — always. A sideways force of unchanging size turns a path’s direction but never its speed, so the particle is steered into a circle, just as the satellite is. Whether the inward part be played by gravity upon a satellite or by a magnetic field upon an electron in a synchrotron ring, it is one piece of circular-motion mathematics in two forms — constant speed, never constant velocity, because the direction never ceases to change.
confucius-en.26: Connects Orbits are built properly in Satellites and circling charges in Circular paths. Area of Study 1 supplies the circular-motion rule both obey — to name which force plays the inward, centripetal part is to reduce many of the year’s hardest problems to one.
confucius-en.27: The third thread: fields are not for explaining nature alone; they may also be used to build. The starting point is the central fact of this Area of Study: a current-carrying wire set across a magnetic field bears a force, named by F = nIlB on formula sheet. Reverse the current, and the force reverses with it.
confucius-en.28: When the wire is bent into a rectangular coil upon an axle between two magnet poles, current travels up one side of the coil and down the other, so the two sides bear equal forces in opposite directions — a turning pair, and the coil rotates. There is one flaw in this design: half a turn later, the same pair of forces would turn it back the other way.
confucius-en.29: The remedy is the split-ring commutator — a copper ring cut in two that reverses the current through the coil every half-turn, so the turning effect always acts the one way. This is a DC motor, the machine within every drill, fan and electric vehicle, and a diagram you will study in detail later. Run it in reverse — the coil spun by wind, water or steam — and a current is driven through the wire instead: that machine is a generator, the whole subject of Area of Study 3.
confucius-en.30: The other great field machine is the particle accelerator, whose working is a two-line summary of this Area of Study: a uniform electric field adds kinetic energy to a charge along a straight line, and a uniform magnetic field bends the charge’s path without altering its speed. The Australian Synchrotron in Clayton joins the two together, holding electrons at more than 99.99 per cent of the speed of light.
confucius-en.31: Connects The motor — with the labelled split-ring commutator diagram examiners ask after in almost every year — is built in Motors & accelerators. Area of Study 3 runs the selfsame hardware in reverse to supply the state with its electricity.
confucius-en.32: The last thread points beyond this Area of Study. Among its dot points is a small act of sorting: classify every field as static or changing, and as uniform or non-uniform. It seems a minor matter; it is the most consequential classification of the year.
confucius-en.33: Changing magnetic fields drive currents in nearby conductors — that single fact, Faraday’s discovery of induction, is how almost every power station on Earth generates electricity, and it is Area of Study 3 in one sentence. Unit 4 then carries the idea further still: a changing electric field creates a changing magnetic field, which recreates the electric field, and the self-sustaining pair detaches from charges and wires altogether and travels through empty space. That travelling ripple of fields is light. The field, brought in as a bookkeeping device for forces, proves to be a thing in its own right.
confucius-en.34: A second loose thread is printed in the study design itself: the circular-path rule for charges holds only while the speed v is much less than c. Synchrotron electrons and GPS satellite clocks both lie outside that condition, and Unit 4’s relativity attends to it. A third: the practical investigation of motor torque — varying the current, the field and the number of loops — is a preparation for the student-designed investigation that ends Unit 4.
confucius-en.35: Connects The accelerator endpoint of this topic is examined in Pass 3. Induction, electromagnetic waves and relativity belong to Area of Study 3 and Unit 4 — signposted now, so that none of them arrives a stranger.
confucius-en.36: The whole Area of Study has now been surveyed once. All that follows is the same content seen at higher resolution, and the book is built so that the resolution before you is always plain.
confucius-en.37: Pass 2 is the core, in five chapters meant to be read the night before each topic is taught: the field idea; gravity and orbits; electric fields; magnetic fields; motors and accelerators. It holds every examinable equation with its formula-sheet status, the standard situations, and quick checks to confirm each idea has been understood.
confucius-en.38: Pass 3 is for the exam season. It walks the sixteen key-knowledge dot points in study-design order — the order from which the exam is written — with the subtleties, the classic traps, the assessors’ advice, and exam-style questions with full solutions. Between passes, depth links like the row below move up and down, and this page stays one click away whenever the detail begins to hide the overall shape.
confucius-en.39: Connects Begin Pass 2 · The field idea when you are ready, and bookmark Pass 3 · Fields and interactions for the exam period.

confucius-zh.0: 子曰：第三单元第二学域之全部——乃至此后大半之年课——皆生于一问：物何以不相触而能动他物？此篇为通览，陈其全貌。今皆不求解，唯览之而已。
confucius-zh.1: 子曰：释手中之手机，则坠。无物可见上而执之——无绳、无手、无气流——然释之之顷，必有所使之向地而加速，其率为 9.8 m s⁻²，无一例外。
confucius-zh.2: 子曰：罗盘之针，封于塑壳之内，于维多利亚之地无往而不指北。气球摩于毛衣，能于一掌之外引人之发。此三小观，同藏一惑：物动而全无相触焉。
confucius-zh.3: 子曰：此惑尝困诸贤。牛顿著万有引力之律，至今犹导航天之器；然其私心，以物隔虚空而相作用为悖理，竟不愿言其何以然。此学域之全——其正名曰 How do things move without contact?——即今人之答也。
confucius-zh.4: 子曰：其答，唯一念耳，曰场，并以一年之果继之。此篇陈其首尾，使详理至于 Pass 2 之时，已入熟识之境矣。
confucius-zh.5: Connects 子曰：场之强者，自十一年级已用之矣：以 9.8 乘诸质量，其单位为 N kg⁻¹——每千克置于其点，所受之牛顿也。Pass 2 chapter 4 立此单位为第三单元之心。
confucius-zh.6: 子曰：其答出于不意之途。法拉第者——装帧之徒，于役间自学其学——穷一八三〇之世，观铁屑环磁石而自成弧。众人徒见其纹，法拉第独进一解：磁石变其周遭之空间，而此变易之空间——彼名之曰 field 之域——实有也，无论有物受其作用与否，皆在焉。
confucius-zh.7: 子曰：今之定义，存其念而精之：场者，于空间每一点皆有其值之物理量也。有场每点持一数——譬如球评之热力图，记一员于 MCG 各处停驻之频，正此类场也。
confucius-zh.8: 子曰：此学域之场，于每点所持者更丰：一矢也。矢之向，示置于其处之适当试物所受力之向；矢之长，示其力之强。场图者，线与矢首交错，即此诸矢之图也——向循其线，强见于线之疏密。
confucius-zh.9: 子曰：绘图一则，深有其义：场线不相交。若二线相会，则其点之场同时有二向，而力不能同时作于二向也。
confucius-zh.10: Connects 子曰：场图者，第二单元力图之所延也：非绘作于一物之诸矢，乃预定每点之矢于先。其精确之义、试物之说与场线之则，启于 What is a field? 之核。
confucius-zh.11: 子曰：第三单元举此图者三。引力之场，源于质量而作于质量。电之场，源于电荷而作于电荷。磁之场，源于动之电荷——线中之电流，或棒磁内电子之绕转自旋——而作于动之电荷。Figure 1.1 并陈其三，而其家族之似，意味深焉：一文法，三方言也。
confucius-zh.12: 子曰：其异之处，正载其理。质量唯一类——无负质量——故引力恒引而不斥，引力之场线皆指向内。以 Figure 1.1 之语，质量纯为壑：线入而不出。
confucius-zh.13: 子曰：电荷有二号。场线出于正荷（源）而入于负荷（壑），二者皆可独存——孤荷者名曰单极。二号者，谓电力可引或可斥也。
confucius-zh.14: 子曰：磁之循则，三者中最奇。未尝见孤立之南北极也：剖棒磁为二，则得二全磁，各具两极，下至于亚原子之粒。磁源唯偶极——源壑永偶——故 Figure 1.1 中磁石之线出于北面，环绕而归于南。
confucius-zh.15: 子曰：此三者之对——引力：一号之单极；电：两号之单极；磁：唯偶极——化三图自三事之当记，为一念之三设矣。Simulation 1.1 许此三者皆可验之。
confucius-zh.16: Connects 子曰：此较成可考之表——源、向、形、引斥、单极与偶极——见于 Comparing the three fields；而试者逐点收之于 Pass 3。
confucius-zh.17: 子曰：此为贯年四绪之首。举书包自地而置于案，乃逆引力之场而作功。其能不灭：储于书包既升之位；包一滑离案，即全数返为动能矣。
confucius-zh.18: 子曰：此学域之诸场，其行皆同。分正负之荷者，储能于其离；释之，则其能复归于动。位于场中，乃储之能——势能也——而此一题，乃复式之记账：能出于一账，入于一账，其总不易。
confucius-zh.19: 子曰：记账之单位，乃当留意之详。引力之储，按每千克之质量计；电之储，按每库仑之荷计——而“每库仑之能”者，自第一单元已用之量，其俗名曰电压。一式，W = qV not on sheet，正述此理：荷 q 经电位差 V 时所变之能——公式表载同义，折入 ½mv² = qV。今皆不必计；此乃第一单元电压之义，逆而读之耳。
confucius-zh.20: 子曰：场不均匀之时，其储以图读之，为力—距图下之面积——即 Units 1&2 用于弹簧之同法也。一账，新储之类耳。
confucius-zh.21: Connects 子曰：引力之储，存于 Gravity and orbits；电之储，存于 Electric fields。第一学域以同账记抛体与碰撞；至于第四单元，光电效应将以电压为度单电子之器。
confucius-zh.22: 子曰：第二绪者，一形也。牛顿想山巅之炮，立于气层之上，平射之。缓弹近落于地；速者落于远；至约 7.9 km s⁻¹，则有奇果生焉——弹之坠向地，恰与地面之曲离同其疾。坠之不已，失之不已：此即轨道之全也。
confucius-zh.23: 子曰：月者，牛顿之炮弹，发于远古者也。每一秒，坠向地约 1.4 mm——其曲径低于本当循之直线之距——而同此一秒，其侧速携之既远，故坠终不使其近。卫星无须引擎以居天：所须者，侧速与一向内之力耳——引力也。
confucius-zh.24: 子曰：此正一俗见于宇航者之误。于国际太空站之高，引力之强犹约 8.7 N kg⁻¹——近其地面之值九成——故谓太空无引力者，谬也。宇航者所以浮者，以站及其内之众皆同坠也：无面施法向之力于人，而法向之力者，正所感为重者也。此态名曰表观失重，而“表观”二字，深有其义。
confucius-zh.25: 子曰：同一算理，现于异形。荷粒横越磁场而行，受一力，名曰 F = qvB on formula sheet，恒指于粒速之正交向。侧力大小恒定，能变径之向而不变其率，故粒被引入圆，正如卫星然。无论向内之任，由引力作于卫星，抑由磁场作于同步环中之电子，皆一圆周算理之二形耳——率恒而速度不恒，以其向无时不变也。
confucius-zh.26: Connects 子曰：轨道之正构，在 Satellites；荷之绕行，在 Circular paths。第一学域供二者所共循之圆周之则——辨何力任向内向心之责，则年中诸难，多化为一矣。
confucius-zh.27: 子曰：第三绪：场非独以释自然也；亦可用以筑器。其始，乃此学域之中心实：载流之线置于磁场之中，受一力，名曰 F = nIlB on formula sheet。逆其流者，逆其力。
confucius-zh.28: 子曰：弯线为方形之圈，绕轴而置于二磁极间，则电流上其一侧而下其另侧，故二侧受等力于相反之向——一转之对也，而圈乃旋。然此制有一缺：半转之后，同一力对将反旋之矣。
confucius-zh.29: 子曰：其救，乃分环换向器——一铜环剖为二，每半转反圈中之流，使其转效恒作于同向。此即直流之马达，钻、扇、电车之内皆有此机，后将详图究之。逆而运之——圈以风、以水、以汽旋之——则电流反被驱于线中：此机名曰发电机，乃第三学域之全题也。
confucius-zh.30: 子曰：另一大场之机，乃粒子加速器，其运乃此学域之二语之括：均匀之电场沿一直线增荷之动能，均匀之磁场弯荷之径而不变其率。克莱顿之澳大利亚同步加速器连此二者，使电子保于光速之 99.99 百分之上。
confucius-zh.31: Connects 子曰：此马达——连其标注之分环换向器之图，试者几每年问之——构于 Motors & accelerators。第三学域运同器于逆，以供一州之电。
confucius-zh.32: 子曰：末绪所指，越乎此学域。其诸点中有一小别之事：辨每场为静或变、为均匀或不均匀。此似末事；然乃年中最关重之别也。
confucius-zh.33: 子曰：变之磁场，驱流于近侧之导体——此一实，即法拉第所发之感应也，乃天下几每电厂生电之由，亦第三学域之一言括也。第四单元更延此念：变之电场生变之磁场，复生电场，而此自持之对，遂离荷与线，行乎虚空。此场之行波，即光也。场者，始为力之记账之器，终证其为独立之物矣。
confucius-zh.34: 子曰：第二散绪，印于学纲之中：荷之圆径之则，唯于速 v 远小于 c 时方保。同步加速器之电子与 GPS 卫星之钟，二者皆出乎此条之外，第四单元相对论应之。其三：马达扭矩之实察——变其流、其场、其圈数——乃终第四单元之学生自设之察之预也。
confucius-zh.35: Connects 子曰：此题加速器之终点，考于 Pass 3。感应、电磁波与相对论，属第三学域与第四单元——今先立其标，使三者至时无一陌生。
confucius-zh.36: 子曰：全学域已一览既毕。自此以往，皆同一内容而析之愈细，而书之所构，使今之析度恒明。
confucius-zh.37: 子曰：Pass 2 为核，凡五章，宜于授课前夜读之：场之念；引力与轨道；电场；磁场；马达与加速器。其载诸可考之式及其公式表之状、诸常见之境，与速检以验每念既明。
confucius-zh.38: 子曰：Pass 3 为试季而设。其循十六要识之点，依学纲之序而行——此即试卷所本之序——陈其微妙、其经典之陷、考官之诫，与试式之题及其全解。诸过之间，深链如下行者上下移；而此页恒在一击之遥，每详理始掩全形之时可返焉。
confucius-zh.39: Connects 子曰：既备则启 Pass 2 · The field idea，并志 Pass 3 · Fields and interactions 以待试期。
